title: The Hollow Forest
author: Andrew Hilton
introduction:
Three weeks ago, Marek sent his last message from the eastern forest: “I think I’ve found the cause.” He was a territorial warden — official access, methodical notes, the kind of man who logged everything. Then nothing.

You’ve been sent to find him. The forest on the empire’s eastern margin is where you start. You have his last known position, a survey map, and a hunting knife. What you don’t have is any certainty about what you’re walking into.

The forest looks wrong. You can’t say exactly how. Not yet.

hunting_knife.name: Hunting Knife
hunting_knife.description: A long-bladed knife, good for skinning and for worse.

flint_steel.name: Flint & Steel
flint_steel.description: Reliable fire-starter. Essential in the wet.

waterskin.name: Waterskin
waterskin.description: Carries water. What you fill it from matters.

forest_map.name: Forest Map
forest_map.description: An official survey map of the eastern margin. Parts of it are wrong.

rope.name: Rope
rope.description: Thirty feet of braided rope. Found in Marek's first camp.

mentors_journal.name: Marek's Journal
mentors_journal.description: Marek's field notes. Careful, methodical, increasingly disturbed.

soil_samples.name: Soil Samples
soil_samples.description: Vials of earth from the contamination sites. The compound is in them.

imperial_documents.name: Imperial Documents
imperial_documents.description: Strategic papers. Proof of intent, not just method.

provisions.name: Provisions
provisions.description: Hard rations. Running low is a slow emergency.

f_1.text:
The margin road ends in stones the colour of old bone, and beyond them the forest begins. You stand at the boundary post â€” a chest-high pillar of weathered granite, the imperial sigil scoured almost smooth by sixty winters of rain â€” and look in.

The trees at the edge are oak and ash and rowan, the same species that line every road in the eastern provinces. They look ordinary. Then the line shifts. Twenty paces in, the canopy darkens, the trunks grow closer, the species mix changes in a way you cannot quite name. There is a quality of silence that you only notice once you stop making your own noise. No wood pigeons. No jay calls. The wind moves the upper branches but no birds come down to feed.

You unfold the forest map. It is creased, official, dated three years ago â€” the eastern margin survey, drawn at one inch to a furlong. Marek's notes are scratched in the corner in his small, careful hand. The last message from him was a single line, sent by post from the margin village four weeks gone:

I think I've found the cause.

Three weeks of silence after that. Then his employer wrote to you, formally, asking whether you would consider going in to look. You agreed before you finished reading the letter.

The map shows two routes from the boundary post toward the operating area where Marek had been documenting the soil. One follows the river south and east through the valley. The other climbs the ridge to the north and tracks the high ground.

You shoulder your pack. The smell coming out of the trees is leaf-mould and cold water and something else underneath. Not bad. Just unfamiliar.

choices.text: Take the valley path along the river.
Take the ridge path along the high ground.

f_5_valley.text:
The valley path drops away from the boundary post in a long shallow gradient, the ground softening underfoot from packed gravel to pine needle to leaf litter. Within a hundred paces the road becomes a track, and within another hundred the track becomes a suggestion â€” a flattening of the undergrowth where feet have passed before, not often.

The river is below you and to the right, more sound than sight. You catch it through gaps: brown water, slow, the colour of strong tea. Your boots find the rhythm of root and stone. The smell is wetter here. Moss on every south face.

The map shows a stone bridge perhaps a mile in, where the path crosses a tributary. Beyond it, the valley widens before narrowing again at the ford.

f_v1.text:
An hour in. The valley is doing what valleys do â€” channelling air and water and light along its axis. The air smells right. The light is doing strange things in the canopy: thin shafts of it angling down, finding patches of fern and bracken to set briefly alight, then moving on. You walk in and out of cold and warmth.

A squirrel watches you from a low branch. It does not flush. It does not chitter. It watches you pass with the still, considering attention of an animal that has decided you are not worth the energy of fear. Six paces on, you look back. It is gone, with no sound of departure.

The path forks. Both branches are vague. The map shows only one.

choices.text: Follow the path that matches the map.
Take the unmarked fork â€” it looks better trodden.

f_v2.text:
You take the marked branch. Within fifty paces it becomes clear the map is right about the route, even if it is wrong about other things: the ground here has been walked, often, by something with the regular stride of a person carrying weight.

The undergrowth thins. You pass a patch of nettles that have been broken at knee height in a long, deliberate sweep â€” someone scything them aside with a stick. The break is not fresh. Two weeks, perhaps. You crouch and look. The cut ends are dry but not yet brown. You think of Marek and how he hated nettles.

f_v2_alt.text:
The unmarked fork pulls you up and to the left, away from the river. The trees thicken. Within two hundred paces the path peters into nothing â€” just a deer trail that thins to a thread and ends at a tangle of bramble. You stand a moment, listening. No water sound. You have lost the river entirely.

You retrace your steps. The fork looks different from this side; you almost miss the way back. Half an hour wasted, the light a little lower. Lesson noted.

choices.text: Take the marked branch this time.

f_v3.text:
The path runs alongside the river for a stretch, close enough now that you can see the water. It moves slowly. Where it slacks against a sandbar, a film sits on the surface â€” not oily, not exactly. A pale matt sheen, the kind milk leaves on a cold cup. You crouch on the bank.

The water smells of nothing. Not the iron-and-leaf smell of clean river water. Just the absence of smell. You touch it with two fingers and bring it to your nose. Nothing. You wipe your hand on your trousers without thinking and immediately wish you had not.

You do not fill the waterskin here.

f_v4.text:
The map shows the stone bridge ahead. You hear the tributary before you see it â€” a faster, brighter sound than the main river, water running over rock. The path drops toward the crossing.

You come around a bend in the trees and stop.

f_v5.text:
Past the broken bridge, the path climbs up the far bank in a series of switchbacks. The going is harder. Your calves notice. Halfway up you stop to drink â€” sparingly, from your own waterskin, the village water you brought in â€” and look back. The river below moves brown and slow. The film catches the light wrong.

A hawk flies along the valley at your eye level, fifty paces out. It is hunting. It does not stoop. It quarters the valley back and forth and finds nothing to dive on. You watch until it passes out of sight beyond the next bend.

f_v6.text:
The path levels onto a flat shelf of ground above the river. You smell something. Animal. Not strong. Not fresh, either.

Fifteen paces off the path, in a patch of disturbed bracken, you see the shape: a deer, lying on its side. The position is wrong. A deer that falls dead does not fall like that. It has lain itself down deliberately and not got up.

You step closer.

f_v7.text:
You leave the deer. It is not your business, you tell yourself, and you only half believe it. The image stays with you for another mile.

The path continues along the upper shelf. The river falls away below. You forage as you walk â€” a habit from years of margin work. There is a scattering of late mushrooms in the leaf litter, but you do not know this part of the forest and you leave them. Better hungry than poisoned.

f_v8.text:
Late afternoon. The shelf path bends back toward the river and brings you to a place where a smaller stream feeds in from the higher ground to your left. The water in this side stream looks different. Clearer. Faster.

The map does not mark it.

choices.text: Follow the side stream uphill briefly.

f_5_ridge.text:
The ridge path climbs steeply for the first quarter-mile, switchbacking up through a stand of birch that gives way to older oak. You feel the climb in your thighs and the cold air in your lungs. The smell up here is different from the valley: drier, with the sharp note of leaf-mould baked by what little autumn sun has reached it.

When you reach the top of the climb you turn and look back. The boundary post is a pale dot below. Beyond it, the open country of the eastern province rolls away in browns and yellows, ordered and flat. From here it looks like a different world.

You turn to face the way ahead. The ridge runs north and east, a long spine of rising ground with the canopy pressing in on both sides.

f_r1.text:
The ridge path is narrow but sound. The footing is good â€” old leaf litter packed down by years of rain and freeze. You can see further from up here, when the trees allow: glimpses of the valley to your right, brown and folded in mist, and far to the north a darker mass where the deep forest begins.

There is wind. Not strong, but constant. It moves the upper branches and brings sounds up from the valley intermittently â€” a crow, the hush of the river, the snap of a branch falling somewhere. The forest has more voice from up here. The silence is patchy.

f_r2.text:
You pass through a stretch of dead ground â€” a half-acre where every tree has lost its leaves out of season. The trunks are not down. They are standing, bare, the bark intact. The ground beneath them is bright with fallen leaves the colour of brass. They came off all at once, recently. There is no wind damage. No fire. No insect bloom you can see.

You walk through it quickly. On the far side, the canopy resumes. You stop and look back at the dead patch. The line is clean: leaves on one side, none on the other, drawn as if by a knife edge along the soil.

f_r3.text:
The ridge widens to a small plateau, perhaps twenty paces across at its broadest. There is a view to the east. You take the chance to sit a moment on a flat slab of stone at the lip and chew a strip of dried meat.

From here you can see the country ahead: the ridge continues for another mile, then the ground drops into a hollow before climbing again into the older forest. You think you see smoke, very faint, from somewhere down in the hollow. A campfire, perhaps. It could be any number of things â€” a charcoal-burner, a forester, Marek himself. You note the bearing.

f_r4.text:
The path narrows. To your left the ground drops sharply into a small re-entrant valley, the slope steep enough that you would not want to fall into it. To your right, gentler ground, dense with bramble. You stay to the centre of the path.

A branch ahead has been broken across the path at chest height. Not snapped by wind â€” cut. The break is clean on one side, jagged on the other. Someone has wrenched it down to mark something. There is no obvious reason.

f_r5.text:
You come to a clearing. The map says a clearing should be here â€” a wide flat space marked with the tiny dot-and-circle that the survey uses for old felling areas, places kept clear by woodsmen for charcoal stacks.

There is no clearing. There is, instead, a stand of birch and ash, the trees thirty feet tall, all of them young. You stand at the edge and look in. The trees are closely spaced and uniform in age. They were not here three years ago when this map was drawn. They were not here, and now they are.

You reckon with the map being wrong.

f_r6.text: Beyond the false clearing, the path resumes â€” a little harder to find now, since whoever last walked it did so before the trees came up. You push through. Your sleeves catch on twigs. You think about the map, folded in your inner pocket, and the survey teams who must have produced it: men with theodolites and chains, walking the ground in measured paces, recording what they saw. You wonder how they would have written down what you have just seen.

f_r7.text:
The ridge takes a long bend to the north. You walk it. There is a rhythm to ridge-walking that the body finds when the mind stops interfering. You let yourself fall into it. Footfall. Breath. The pack on your shoulders. The cold of the air on the side of your face.

You pass a lone holly tree, its berries still green, and beneath it the white feathers of a wood pigeon scattered in a tight circle. A goshawk kill, by the look. Days old.

f_r8.text:
Late afternoon. The light has thickened. The ridge begins to drop, slowly at first, then more steeply, into the hollow where you saw the smoke earlier. You can no longer see the smoke. Either it has gone out, or you are too low to see over the trees, or you imagined it.

The path forks. Down into the hollow, or along a contour that seems to skirt it.

choices.text: Skirt the hollow on the contour path.

f_r9.text:
The hollow path is gentle and the smell of woodsmoke does come up to meet you, faint, then unmistakable. But by the time you reach the bottom there is no fire. There is, instead, a circle of stones with cold ash in the middle of it, and the bracken pressed flat in a man-shape beside it. Someone slept here last night. They have moved on.

You crouch by the ashes. They are still warm in the centre when you push your fingers in. Very recent. You stand up quickly and look around. Nothing. The hollow is quiet.

f_r10.text:
The contour path keeps you out of the hollow. You can see the tops of the trees in it as you walk above. Once, you think you see movement â€” a flash of dark colour through the canopy, the wrong size and shape for a deer. It does not repeat.

The path bends back inward and brings you down a long slope of bracken to a place where the ridge ends and the forest floor takes over again. You can no longer see far ahead. The trees close in.

f_10_valley.text:
Where the bridge should be, there is no bridge.

The map is clear: a stone bridge, two arches, dressed limestone. There are abutments â€” squared blocks of limestone, mossed, half-collapsed on the near bank. There is a single pier in the middle of the tributary, a rough stub of stone with grass growing on its top. There is no span. There has not been one for a long time. The masonry that should be lying in the water is not lying in the water. The river is shallow here and you would see it.

There is no rubble. There is no sign that the bridge ever fell. There are only the abutments and the pier, as if the bridge between them had been lifted away whole and carried off.

The tributary itself is shallow â€” knee deep at most, fast over rounded stones. You can wade.

f_10_ridge.text:
You push through the new growth and come out the other side onto the path. You stop and consider what you have just walked through.

If the survey teams were here three years ago â€” and the dating on the map is unambiguous â€” then this stand of trees has come up in three years. The trees are thirty feet tall. Birch and ash do not do that. Not in three years. Not in twenty.

You look at the map again. The little dot-and-circle. The careful pen-strokes of the surveyor. Either the surveyor lied, which is possible, or the trees grew, which should not be possible, or someone has planted thirty-foot trees in a clearing, which is absurd.

You fold the map. The certainty you walked in with has gone.

f_15.text:
The deer is a roe doe, perhaps two years old. Healthy, by the look â€” the coat thick, the body in good condition for the season. She has not been killed. There is no wound, no torn throat, no broken neck. She has simply lain herself down.

No predator has fed on her. The flies are present but not swarming â€” perhaps a dozen, dull and slow. You expect more. In the warm afternoon, on a fresh deer, you should be brushing them off your face. There are not enough flies. Something is wrong with the flies, or with the deer, or both.

You crouch and look at the ground around her body. The leaf litter is undisturbed except where her own weight has pressed it. And in a rough oval the size of a wagon wheel, centred on her, the soil colour is wrong. Not dark loam. A pale grey-green. As if it has been bleached, or salted, or drained of something.

You stand up and step back. You note it. You do not have a word for it. You move on.

f_18.text:
The light is beginning to fail. You can feel it in the air before you see it in the sky â€” a particular cooling, a flatness in the colours. To the west, low cloud is moving in, and with it the smell of rain coming. Not now, but tonight.

You need shelter. You find a place: a leaning oak with a hollow at the base, the ground around it dry, a fallen branch you can drag across to make a windbreak. Twenty minutes' work and you have a serviceable lean-to. You crawl in and out checking the angle of any rain that comes.

The question is fire. The flint and steel is in your pack. There is dead wood within easy walking distance â€” birch bark, dry twigs, a couple of split branches you can manage. With fire, the night will be survivable. Without, it will be cold and wet and you will not sleep well.

choices.text: Use the flint and steel. Make a fire.

f_18_fire.text:
You gather wood while there is still light enough to see what you are picking up. Birch bark from a fallen log, dry as paper. A handful of last-year's bracken, cracked between your hands. Twigs from the underside of a fir, where they stay dry in any weather. You build the fire small, the way a careful man does â€” a tight cone, the bark at the base, the bracken stuffed beneath.

The flint sparks on the third strike. The bracken catches. The bark catches. You feed it twigs.

It is a small fire, and it is the difference between two kinds of night.

f_18_nofire.text:
You decide against the fire. The wood is more damp than it looks, your hands are tired, the light is going. You eat cold provisions and crawl into the lean-to with your coat drawn around you and your pack against your back.

The rain comes after dark. You hear it first in the canopy, a hush spreading from the west, and then it reaches the lean-to and you discover the angle of your roof was not quite as good as you thought.

test_luck.lucky_text: You sleep in fits but you sleep. The rain stops before midnight. You wake stiff and cold but functional.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The rain finds you. By the small hours your coat is wet through and you are shivering hard enough that you cannot sleep. You sit up, hugging your knees, watching the dark, waiting it out.

f_20_warm.text:
You feed the fire down to a steady glow and sit with your back against the oak, the heat of it on your shins, the smoke going up cleanly into the canopy. The rain when it comes goes around the edge of your shelter, not into it.

You notice something while you are tending the fire. The wood from one of the logs you split â€” a piece of alder, the bark grey, the inner wood pale â€” burns wrong. It catches. It produces flame. But the smoke from it carries a chemical sharpness, a thin acrid note that is not woodsmoke. It is the smell of something underneath the wood.

You pick the alder out of the fire with a stick and let it lie aside. The remaining wood burns clean. You add it sparingly. You catalogue what you have noticed: the alder by the shelter burns wrong. You file it. You sleep.

f_20_cold_lucky.text:
Morning comes grey and damp. You unfold yourself from the lean-to with the slow effort of a body that has lost more heat than it should have. You stamp your feet, swing your arms, get your blood moving. Your coat is half-dry, your boots damp. You eat. You go on.

The day has the quality of a day after a bad night: a thin, fragile alertness, easily disturbed.

f_20_cold_unlucky.text:
Dawn finds you cold to the bone. Your coat is heavy with rain. Your boots squelch when you stand. You force yourself through the morning ritual â€” eat, pack, walk â€” but every action is slower than it should be. Your hands are clumsy on the laces. You drop a strap twice before you get the pack right.

You will pay for this night for the rest of the day.

f_21.text:
Some time before dawn â€” the sky still a deep blue, the fire down to coals â€” you wake. Not from a dream. From a sound, or the absence of one.

You lie still and listen. The rain has stopped. The forest is quiet, but it is the wrong quiet. Then you hear it: a careful step at the edge of your firelight. The shift of leaf-litter under a paw. Another step.

You turn your head, slowly. Yellow eyes catch the embers and reflect.

A wolf. Lean, full-grown, standing perhaps fifteen paces out, its head low, its ears forward. It is not crouched to charge. It is investigating. The fire has drawn it. Wolves do not normally come this close to fires.

choices.text: Stay perfectly still and let it watch.
Stand up slowly and add wood to the fire to drive it back.
Reach for the hunting knife and prepare to fight.

f_21_still.text:
You do not move. You breathe through your mouth, slowly. The wolf stands at the edge of the light for what feels like a long minute and is probably less. It watches you, you watch it. Its ribs show. Not starving. Lean.

Then, with the unhurried economy of a healthy animal, it turns and walks back into the trees. No sound after. No second pass. You lie awake until the first grey of morning, your hand on the knife the whole time.

f_21_fire.text:
You sit up slowly. The wolf's eyes track the movement but it does not retreat. You reach for a piece of split wood and lay it on the coals. The wood is dry. It catches almost at once and the flame leaps. The wolf flinches, takes two steps back, and stops.

You add another piece. The fire grows. The wolf watches the flames with a flat dislike, then turns and is gone, in the silent way of a wolf that has decided it is not interested.

test_luck.lucky_text: It does not return. You sit by the high fire until dawn, more awake than you would like, but unharmed.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Twenty minutes later you hear it again â€” the careful step, the shift of weight. It has not gone far. This time it comes in.

f_21_fight.text:
You take the knife from your belt. The blade is short but the edge is good. You stand, slowly, putting the fire between yourself and the wolf.

The wolf reads the change in posture and does what wolves do when challenged at a fire: it commits. It comes in low and fast.

f_21_combat.text:
The branch is not enough. It comes in low and fast — no hesitation, no posturing, the way a hungry animal moves when it has decided. You drop the burning stick and get the knife up between you.

It is not a long fight.

f_21_won.text:
The wolf goes down hard against your shelter pole, breath rasping, then still. You stand over it with the knife slick. It is a young male, lean, the coat in good condition. It has no obvious sickness. You wonder why it pressed a fire it should have avoided.

You do not skin it. You drag the body twenty paces from your camp and leave it. You are awake the rest of the night.

f_21_fled.text:
You break and run from the firelight, the wolf after you for ten paces, then less, then nothing. When you stop and turn, the dark is empty. You make your way back to the shelter from a different angle, knife drawn. The fire has burned down. The wolf is gone.

You do not sleep again.

f_22.text:
Morning. You break camp at first light and find the path again within a few minutes. The forest has a clean, washed quality after the rain. The smells are sharper. Birds â€” a single robin somewhere, then nothing else. The robin sounds out of place.

A hundred paces along the path, you see the first mark. A cut on the trunk of an oak, at chest height, the shape of a chevron. The wood is fresh, pale, the sap not yet darkened. It is not a blaze of the kind hunters use. It is too neat â€” the angles too consistent, the depth controlled.

Thirty paces on, another, on a beech. Same height, same shape. Then another. Then another.

They are spaced at regular intervals. Survey spacing. They are leading somewhere, and they are not Marek's. Marek used cairns: a small stack of stones at the base of a tree, easily missed, easily kicked apart. He did not cut trees.

choices.text: Ignore them. Stay on your own course.

f_22_follow.text:
You decide to follow the markers. They lead you off the path, gently, in a curving line that takes you up a slight rise to the north. Whoever cut them was not in a hurry, and not concerned with the easiest going. They wanted to be findable, but only by someone who knew to look.

At the twentieth or so marker, the chevron changes. A small horizontal bar is added beneath it. Three trees later, a vertical stroke through it. You do not know the cipher. But you understand that this is one.

The last mark is on a young birch. Hanging from a branch above it, in a waxed canvas sack, is a cache.

f_22_cache.text:
You unhook the sack. It is heavy. The canvas is in good condition, the wax fresh, the seams machine-stitched. You loosen the drawstring.

Inside: a parcel of dried meat wrapped in oiled paper. A small loaf of dense travel bread. A tin canteen, full and sealed. And in a side pocket, a folded note in a hand you do not know â€” short, terse: For S. Pickup confirmed. Move to next at marker chain D.

You hold the canteen up to the light. There is a small stamp pressed into the metal at the base. An imperial mark, the kind used on quartermaster's stores, easy to miss if you are not looking for it.

The cache was placed. For someone. By someone with imperial supply.

choices.text: Take what you can carry. Leave the note where it was.
Take only the canteen. Leave the rest.

f_22_after.text:
You take the food and the canteen and re-hang the empty sack as you found it, with the note tucked back inside. The hand it was written in is not Serrin's, you think, though you have not yet met Serrin and you do not know why his name is in your head. You have not read it. The S could be anything.

You continue on, heavier in the pack and lighter in the conscience than you would like.

f_22_after_light.text:
You take only the canteen. You tell yourself that water is the most important thing and that you are leaving the food for whoever it was meant for. You hang the sack as you found it.

The canteen is heavier than it should be in your pack. You walk on.

f_22_after_none.text:
You replace everything. You hang the sack carefully and step back. Whatever this cache is for, it is not yours. You return to your own line of march.

You are hungrier than you would like to be, and you feel cleaner than you would like to admit.

f_22_ignore.text:
You do not follow the markers. Whoever made them is doing imperial work â€” the spacing, the discipline of the cuts â€” and your business is Marek, who did not cut trees. You stay on your line.

The markers continue past you for a few hundred paces and then peel off to the north. You watch the last of them disappear into the trees and feel a small relief that is also, perhaps, a small foolishness.

f_22_alone.text:
An hour beyond the last marker, the path you are following thins to nothing and you have to navigate by the lay of the land â€” the slope of the ground, the angle of the light, the run of the streams as the map (more or less) describes them. It is slower work and demands more attention.

At midday, you stop to eat the last of your road bread. There is a small clearing beside a fallen log. As you sit, a cock pheasant breaks from the bracken twenty paces off and goes up in a clatter of wings. Your hand goes to the knife before you realise. Pheasants are still pheasants. The forest is mostly the forest.

You eat. You go on.

f_28.text:
Mid-afternoon. The path â€” or your best approximation of one â€” drops into a shallow bowl of ground sheltered on three sides by older oak. There is the smell of old fire. You stop.

It is a camp. Not occupied. Not recent â€” the ash in the fire-scar is grey-white and rain-washed, and the bracken bedding has flattened and begun to rot at the edges. Three weeks, you think, perhaps four. The right age.

You step into it carefully and stand a moment, looking. Then you start to read it the way you would read a page.

The fire-scar is rectangular, not round â€” the way Marek made fires, longwise, for cooking on a flat stone. The shelter line was tied off on a sapling with a tautline hitch â€” you can see where the bark has been compressed in the right pattern. There is a square of trampled bracken where his bedroll lay, the head end against the slope so the blood would not pool in his face if he were ill. He was particular about that.

You crouch by the fire-scar. Marek was here. He stayed several days. He left in his own time, taking his gear with him. He left nothing he would have wanted left.

Except one thing. Hung from the lower branch of the nearest oak, coiled neatly and tied off with a clove hitch, is a length of rope. Forty feet of it. Good rope, hemp, well kept.

You stand looking at it for some time. Either he forgot it, which is not like him, or he left it for someone. For a follower. For you.

choices.text: Take the rope and search the camp more carefully.
Take the rope and move on.

f_28a.text:
You take the rope and stow it. Then you walk the camp slowly, in expanding circles from the fire-scar.

Ten paces north, in the leaf-litter, a small square of bark has been peeled from a young ash and replaced. You lift it. Beneath, scratched into the white wood, is a single word in Marek's small careful hand: NORTH. An arrow, pointing further into the forest. Below the arrow, a mark like a circle with a line through it â€” his shorthand for danger.

You replace the bark.

f_28b.text: You take the rope and you move on. You do not search further. You do not want to find anything that would change the picture you have of Marek leaving this camp on his own two feet, in his own time, alive.

f_28c.text:
Beyond the camp, the ground rises again into a long oak gallery â€” old trees, thirty feet apart, the canopy high and the underwood almost absent. Walking is easy here. You make good time.

You do not see another animal for an hour. Not a squirrel, not a deer, not a bird. The gallery is empty. Only the trees, moving slightly when the wind reaches them.

f_30.text: The oak gallery ends at a small stream. The stream is bright. Faster than the river. The water, where you crouch to it, smells of leaf and stone â€” the smell river water should have. You fill the waterskin. It is a small relief that you did not know you were looking for.

f_32.text:
You leave the main path and follow the side stream up the slope. It is steeper than it looks and your calves burn within fifty paces, but you push on. The stream gets clearer the higher you go. After a quarter of a mile of climbing through alder and hazel, the trees open and you find the spring.

It comes out of a cleft in a slab of old rock, cold to the touch, falling perhaps three feet into a basin worn into the stone. The water in the basin is so clear you can see the individual grains of sand on its bottom. You can smell the water â€” clean, mineral, faintly cold.

You empty your waterskin and refill it from the basin. The first mouthful tastes like winter.

f_32a.text: You scramble back down to the main path with cold knees and a full skin. You look back once at the line of alder marking the spring's course. You think you will remember the way. You hope so.

f_36.text:
The forest changes character through the late afternoon. The trees grow further apart, but larger; the undergrowth thins; the light gets in. You feel, briefly, that you are in an ordinary wood. Then you notice that the light is wrong â€” it is too late in the day to be this colour. The slant is wrong. You check the sun through the canopy. It is where it should be. The light is doing something else.

You shake the thought off and walk on.

f_38.text:
You smell the camp before you see it. Woodsmoke â€” proper woodsmoke, with the tang of bacon underneath. Fat in a pan. You stop on the path and stand listening, and you hear a man laugh, easy and quiet, and another voice answer in a normal speaking tone.

You come around a stand of hazel and they see you the same moment you see them.

Three of them. A camp set up properly: oilskin tarp, neat fire-pit, packs racked against a log. Good gear. A canvas-and-leather field case open beside the senior man, with what looks like surveyor's tools laid out on the cloth â€” a small theodolite, a brass chain, a notebook in oilskin.

"Hello," says the senior man. He is perhaps fifty, lean, weather-worn, with a beard kept short and grey eyes that do not quite seem to settle on you. He stands up unhurriedly. "You'll be the second one through this week."

The other two â€” a younger woman, dark-haired, and a heavy-set man with the build of a soldier â€” look up and nod, friendly, unsurprised. The woman has a piece of bacon on a stick over the embers.

"Serrin," the senior man says, putting out a hand. "Imperial Survey, Eastern Margins. Yourself?"

choices.text: Give your name and accept his handshake.

f_38_meet.text:
You give your name. His hand is dry and his grip is brief. He says, "Pleased," and means it, or seems to. He gestures to the fire.

"You're welcome to the warmth and a piece of bacon while you decide whether you're staying or moving on. There's good light for another two hours. The next decent camping ground is an hour ahead at most. We're set up here for the night either way."

The younger woman â€” she introduces herself as Mira â€” hands you a tin mug of something hot. It is tea, sweet, strong. The heavy-set man does not give a name, only a nod. Serrin sees you notice.

"That's Hask," he says, smiling slightly. "He doesn't talk much. It's a virtue in our line of work."

You drink the tea. Serrin asks, mildly, conversationally, what brings you in this far. He has the careful manner of a man who is interested in your answer but would not press for it if you preferred not to give it. He mentions, in passing, that they have seen sign of someone three days ahead of you, heading northeast â€” a man on his own, moving steadily, knowing his way.

"Friend of yours, perhaps," Serrin says.

choices.text: Stay and camp with them tonight.
Thank them and move on while there is light.

f_38_camp.text:
You stay. The afternoon turns into evening with the easy rhythm of a good camp. Hask cuts firewood without speaking, in clean efficient strokes. Mira cooks: bacon, then a stew of dried beef and barley, then tea again, and a small ration of brandy from a tin flask Serrin produces with a slight ceremonial air.

Serrin asks questions in the way of a man who genuinely wants to know things. What did you make of the forest at the margin. Did you find the bridge. (You glance at him at that, but his face shows only friendly interest.) Did you see anything in the dead patches that surprised you. He listens to your answers with attention, sometimes nodding, sometimes asking a follow-up that suggests he has been here longer than you and seen more than you, and is only checking what you noticed.

It does not feel like an interrogation. It feels like a conversation between professionals. Mira contributes occasionally â€” a wry comment, a small joke. Hask sharpens his knife and listens.

When you sleep, by their fire, under the oilskin awning, you sleep well. The first good sleep since you crossed the boundary post.

choices.text: Wake to morning and move on.

f_38_morning.text:
You wake to the smell of coffee. Real coffee, ground and brewed, the kind only a well-funded survey team would carry into the field. Serrin pours you a tin cup of it without asking. The day is bright.

They are packing as you eat. They are heading further east along the margin, mapping a section of the dead patches. "We may be a few days behind you," Serrin says, "but we'll be in country by the same fork. If anything goes amiss for you, you might bear that in mind."

It is not quite an offer of help. It is closer to a placeholder for one. He shakes your hand again and Hask gives you, unexpectedly, half a loaf of dense travel bread wrapped in a cloth.

You shoulder your pack and go on.

f_38_leave.text:
You thank them for the tea and stand. Serrin's grey eyes do not quite settle on you, but his smile is warm enough.

"Of course," he says. "Light's the great currency out here. Don't waste it on us. I'll note your direction in the journal â€” easterly, toward the deep forest. If anything goes amiss, that's where we'll start looking."

It is gracefully said. You find you do not know how to refuse the gesture without rudeness, and so you accept it: yes, easterly, toward the deep forest, looking for a friend.

Mira gives you a small smile. Hask does not look up from the fire.

You walk on. Within ten paces you cannot smell the smoke any more. Within fifty, you cannot hear them.

f_42.text:
An hour past the survey camp. The light is falling fast now and you need to make your own shelter again, less practiced this time. You find a clump of fir with a dry hollow at its base and crawl in. It is not a good camp. It will do.

You eat a cold supper. You think about Serrin's eyes â€” the way they did not quite settle. The way he said the word friend.

choices.text: Sleep, badly. Move on at first light.

f_45.text:
Mid-morning. The forest changes again, and this time the change is unambiguous.

The trees ahead are larger. Older. Thirty paces further on, you cannot see the ground for the leaf-fall. The trunks press closer together. The canopy, which should be denser given the size of the trees, is thinner. Light gets through in a way it should not. You crane your neck.

The canopy is thinner because the trees are dying from the top down. Crown dieback. The upper branches of half the trees you can see are leafless and pale, the bark sloughing in long strips. The lower canopy â€” the working canopy, the leaves that take in light â€” is doing its best. But the crowns are gone. From the air, this stretch of forest would look healthy. From beneath it, you can see what is happening.

There is a smell here. Not strong. The ground smell of the deer, cold-bright and chemical, but spread thinly through the air. You taste it on the back of your tongue.

The map shows nothing unusual at this point. The map is, as you have learned, a kind of fiction.

f_48.text:
You stand at the threshold.

It is not a line, exactly. It is a deepening. The trees grow taller. The light grows thinner. The path becomes a suggestion of a path, and then less than that. The smell on the back of your tongue grows specific.

You check the knife on your belt. You check the rope coiled at the side of your pack. You take a swallow of water from the skin and re-stopper it carefully. You fold the map and put it away. The map is no use here. The map was never any use here.

You look back once. You can still see the way you came, in patches, between the trees. The ridge in the far distance. The pale strip of sky over the valley. You fix it in your mind because you suspect you will not see it the same way again.

Then you turn forward, and you cross.

f_extra_1.text: The ground here is soft with moss. You walk for a long stretch without speaking aloud, which you have not realised you have been doing, in fits and starts, since you crossed the boundary post. You stop yourself from doing it and the silence comes in around you with the small weight of an animal settling on your chest.

f_extra_2.text: You cross a fallen beech, the trunk furred with moss, the inside of it hollow and soft when you put your hand in. A wood-mouse darts out across your boot. You feel a small, ridiculous pleasure at it: an ordinary animal doing an ordinary thing.

f_extra_3.text:
The fog comes up out of the valley in the early afternoon, drifting up the slopes in slow rolls. Within minutes the trees twenty paces ahead are softened to grey shapes. Within an hour you cannot see thirty feet.

You slow your pace. You navigate by the slope of the ground.

f_extra_4.text: You stop to forage at the edge of a clearing where late blackberries cling to a thicket. You eat what you can pick without thorns. They are sweet, slightly past their best, and ordinary. You stand among them eating with your fingers stained dark, listening to the absence of birdsong in a place where birds should be feeding on these same berries, and you understand, with a cold small clarity, that something is wrong with the birds.

f_extra_5.text:
A hawk in the upper branches. You see it before it sees you and you stop. It is an immature goshawk, brown-flecked, hunched on a horizontal branch. You wait for it to flush.

It does not flush. You watch it for a full minute. Its head turns once, slowly, to track something at the edge of its vision. Otherwise it does not move. It is watching the forest floor with the patient attention of a thing that intends to be there for hours.

You pass beneath it. It does not look at you.

f_extra_6.text:
You think about the message. I think I've found the cause. Marek was not given to drama. He was given to careful sentences in which every word carried weight. The cause of what, he did not say, because he assumed you would know â€” and you did know, in the broad sense: the eastern forest was sick. It had been sick for a generation. The empire had sent surveys. Marek had gone in privately, on his own coin, on his own time, because the official surveys were not asking the right questions.

You wonder what the right questions are.

f_extra_7.text:
A patch of mushrooms at the base of a beech â€” fly agarics, the red caps bright as a wound in the leaf-litter. You leave them. You know what they do. You walk on, and a hundred paces later you come upon another patch, and another beyond that. A long line of them, running through the forest in a curve as if marking something underground.

You make a small detour to keep clear.

f_extra_8.text: Late afternoon light through high cloud. The colour of the forest goes flat and the temperature drops a degree. You feel it on the back of your neck before you notice it on your hands. You add a layer.

f_extra_9.text:
You find a cairn beside the path â€” three flat stones, stacked, the topmost one with a small chip out of one corner. It is one of Marek's. Your throat tightens, foolishly, at the sight of it.

He was here. Heading the same direction you are. You add a fourth stone to the cairn before you walk on, and you do not know why you do it.

f_extra_10.text: You navigate by sight of the sun through the canopy. The path you have been on has thinned to deer-trail. You match the contours of the map, more or less, and the map agrees, more or less, and you trust both of them less than you would like.

f_extra_11.text: A thin rain begins, then stops. Then begins again. You walk through it. Your hat keeps the worst from your face. The smell of the forest changes with the wet â€” leaf-mould rises, the alder has its faint chemical edge, the moss puts out the green smell of moss.

f_extra_12.text:
You sit a moment on a log to rest your legs. There is a beetle on the bark beside you, dark green, the size of a thumbnail. It is not moving. You watch it for half a minute. It is alive â€” you can see the slight movement of its mouthparts â€” but it is not moving. You touch it gently with the tip of a twig. It does not respond.

You move the twig away. The beetle stays as it was. You stand up and walk on.

d_1.text:
The threshold is behind you. The forest you walk into now is older.

The trees are larger here, the trunks broader than two of you laid end to end. The canopy closes overhead and the light changes quality — not darker, exactly, but thinner, as if filtered through something fine. The ground is deep with leaf-fall. Your boots make almost no sound.

You pass a stand of mature oak. The bark on three of them is lifting away from the trunks in long sheets, curling like old paper. Beneath the lifted bark the wood is pale and dry. There is no insect tracery, no fungal bloom. Only the dryness, like something cured.

You stop and listen. The sound of the forest is wrong. Not silence — there is a small wind moving in the upper branches, and somewhere ahead a woodpecker is working at a dead tree — but the sound is too thin. There should be more. Birdsong at this hour. The small movements of mice and squirrels in the leaf-litter. The hum of insects at the canopy gaps.

You listen to the woodpecker for a while. It works steadily, methodically. It finds nothing. Whatever was in the tree is already gone.

choices.text: Push on along what remains of the path.
Examine the lifted bark more closely.

d_2.text:
You crouch by the nearest oak and lift a sheet of bark away from the trunk. It comes free in your hand without resistance, light as old vellum.

The inner surface is dry. There is no sap, no moisture, no smell of resin. The wood beneath is pale and slightly powdery to the touch. You rub a little between thumb and finger. It is the texture of fine ash.

You lay the bark back against the trunk. It does not hold. It falls into the leaf-litter at your feet.

You wipe your hand on your trousers and stand.

d_3.text:
The path is intermittent now — a suggestion through the leaf-fall, broken by deadfall and reasserted thirty paces on. You follow what remains of it by feel as much as by sight.

The ground rises gently. Through the trunks ahead you can hear water — a low, continuous sound that resolves, as you walk, into the voice of a river.

d_4.text:
You unfold the map and find your approximate position by the rise and the watercourse. The river is marked. So is a bridge, half a day downstream of where you must be.

You study the path-markings. The map shows a single forester's track running roughly along the line you are walking. It does not show the deadfall. It does not show the dying oaks. The map was drawn by someone who walked this in summer, in a year when the forest was still itself.

You fold the map away.

d_5.text:
The slope steepens. You go down it sideways, one hand on the trunks of the smaller trees for balance. Halfway down you stop.

There is a deer below you, lying in the leaf-litter at the foot of the slope. It is not moving. It is not, you think after a long moment of looking at it, recently dead — the body has the slack settled look of something that has been there for days, but the carrion-smell is faint, and there are no flies. There should be flies.

The deer is intact. No wound. No predator damage. It lay down here and it stopped.

choices.text: Skirt around it and continue down to the river.

d_6.text:
You step closer. The deer is a young hind, a yearling. Its eyes are open and dry. The flank is unmarked. You crouch and look at the muzzle. There is a faint dark staining around the nostrils and the corners of the mouth. Not blood. Something more like the residue left when water dries on metal.

You do not touch it.

You stand and back away, and the smell on the back of your tongue is the smell that was on the air at the dying oaks.

d_8.text:
The river runs broad and shallow over a stony bed. On the map it is marked as a reliable water source.

Up close, the colour of the water is slightly off. Not dramatically — not the ochre of iron, not the white of glacial silt. Just not quite right. A faint cast at the edges of the riffles, where the water slows. You watch a long moment before you can name what it reminds you of: the colour of weak tea, gone cold.

You kneel and put your hand in. The water is cold and the smell on your wet hand is faint but present. The same smell.

choices.text: Refill from the clean spring you mapped earlier instead — you will not drink this.
Approach the river bank to drink.

d_8_clean.text:
You know this water. You have seen it before, upstream, where the springs feed clean.

You backtrack a quarter mile to a feeder you remember from your earlier crossings — a small cold seep coming straight out of stone, the kind of water Marek taught you to trust. You fill the skin slowly, letting the silt settle, and stopper it.

You rejoin the river path with the weight of full water against your hip. The river beside you moves the wrong colour and you do not look at it.

d_8_river.text:
Your throat is dry. The river is right there. The wrongness is faint — a cast in the colour, a smell that might only be wet stone.

You crouch at the edge with the waterskin open. Whether or not you drink is a thing you decide in the next moment.

test_luck.lucky_text: Something stops you. The smell, sharper now with your face this close to the water. The way a fish would not be in this. You stand up without filling the skin and step back. Your throat will hurt for a few hours. It will pass.
test_luck.unlucky_text: You drink. The water has almost no taste. You fill the skin. Half a mile further on the first wave of nausea finds you — a low, slow turning under the breastbone. You keep walking. There is nothing else to do.

d_8_didnt_drink.text: You walk on with the empty skin and a dry mouth. The river beside you continues its low conversation with the stones. You do not look at it.

d_8_drank.text:
The nausea sits in you and does not leave. You walk through it. You stop once and crouch over the leaf-litter, but nothing comes up. Your hands are cold.

After a time the worst of it dulls into a low, persistent unwellness. It does not go away.

d_10.text:
The path follows the river downstream. The trees here are thinner, more recent — second growth, by the look of them, on what was once cleared ground. The understorey is sparse. You see no birds.

A hundred paces on you find a place where someone has been. A patch of trampled fern, a scuff in the moss where boots have turned. Not recent — yesterday at the earliest, perhaps two days. You crouch and look. One set of prints. Larger than yours. Heading the same way.

choices.text: Step off the path and parallel it through the trees.

d_11.text:
You move twenty paces into the trees and walk parallel to the path, keeping it visible at the edge of your peripheral vision. The going is harder — more deadfall, more ducking under low branches — but you are not on the trail.

You are not sure why you have done this. The prints were old. There is no one ahead of you that you know of. But the unease has been building since the river, and the unease wants you off the obvious line.

After a quarter mile the trees open and you see the path again, and beyond the path, the river bend, and the place where the bridge should be.

choices.text: Rejoin the path and approach the crossing.

d_12.text:
The bridge is gone.

The map shows a sturdy timber bridge here, the kind a logging crew would build. What is here instead is a debris dam — a snarl of fallen trunks and brush wedged across the narrows, twenty feet wide and head-high in the middle. Water spills through it in a dozen small white falls. The original bridge timbers are part of it, you think, near the bottom.

The debris is crossable. You can see the line — two long trunks at the upstream face, mostly stripped of bark, slick-looking. The current beneath is fast and dark.

The far bank is twelve paces off.

choices.text: Tie the rope as a handline and cross carefully.
Cross without aid — pick your way across the trunks.
Look for a ford further downstream.

d_12_rope.text:
You uncoil the rope. There is a stub of broken bridge-timber jutting from the dam at the near side, and a forked branch at the far end of the upstream trunk — enough to anchor a handline. You make the throw twice before it sets.

With the rope taut at waist-height you cross slowly, three points of contact at all times. The trunks shift under your weight but the line holds. Halfway across you stop because the spray has soaked your trousers and your hands are numb. You make yourself breathe. You go on.

On the far bank you coil the rope wet and heavy back into your pack. Your hands are shaking but you are across.

choices.text: Continue into the trees on the far side.

d_12_unaided.text:
You step out onto the upstream trunk. It is slicker than it looked. You go in a low crouch, hands on the trunk, feeling for the dry patches with your boots.

Four steps out the trunk shifts under you.

test_luck.lucky_text: You drop flat and grip with both arms. The trunk settles. You wait for the shake in your legs to stop, then keep going, slower. You reach the far bank wet to the knees and trembling, but unharmed.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Your foot goes. You catch yourself with one elbow against the trunk as the leg drops into the water and the current pulls. You haul yourself out and forward in a panic of scraped wood and freezing water. You make the far bank. Your trousers are soaked, there is a long graze along your forearm, and one of your boots has lost a strap.

d_13.text:
You walk downstream a quarter mile, looking for shallows. The river runs against a steep cut bank for most of that distance, and where it eases there is no good crossing — soft mud, deep pools, no clear bed.

You come to a place where the water broadens over a stretch of gravel. It looks fordable. You wade in to mid-thigh before the current takes hold of your boots and tells you it is faster than it looked.

choices.text: Push across — commit to the ford.
Back out and return to the debris dam.

d_13_ford.text:
You set your weight against the current and walk. The water rises to your waist. You keep your eyes on the far bank and your feet feeling for the bed.

Half-way across a submerged stone shifts under you and you go down to your chest before you catch your balance. Cold all through. You walk the last six paces with your teeth clamped and your fingers losing feeling, and you fall up the gravel of the far bank on hands and knees.

choices.text: Strip the worst of the wet, warm yourself, and go on.

d_12_back.text: You return upstream to the debris dam. Your legs are wet to the knee and your patience is thin.

choices.text: Tie the rope as a handline and cross carefully.

d_15.text:
On the far side of the river the forest changes again.

The trees are smaller here, more uniform — the look of a stand that grew in after a long-ago clearance and was never thinned. The understorey is sparse. The leaf-fall is thin. The ground feels harder under your boots.

The path picks up again, more confidently than before. You follow it. Somewhere ahead is — by the map and by the rumour you came in on — Marek's working country. The watershed he was studying. The place his last letters mentioned.

The path curves. You walk.

choices.text: Stop to forage — your provisions will not last forever.

d_16.text:
You stop in a small clearing where the leaf-fall is thin and the ground is dappled with light. You look for what you know — hazel, beech mast, the bracket fungus that takes well in stew, the late berries on the thorn at the edges.

You walk in widening circles for a quarter hour. There is nothing. The hazel you find is old and bare. The thorn carries no fruit. You turn one of the rotting logs at the clearing edge and there are no grubs, no woodlice, no centipedes. Only the soft pale wood, slightly powdery to the touch.

You stand a long time looking at the empty log.

choices.text: Move on. There is nothing here.

d_18.text:
The path drops into a shallow valley. The trees on the slopes are mixed — beech, oak, some hornbeam — and the air is briefly cooler. You hear, a little to the south, the sound of someone breathing.

You stop. You listen. Not breathing — sobbing. Quiet, exhausted sobbing. A man's voice, by the pitch of it.

choices.text: Skirt wide around it and continue along the path.

d_19.text:
You step off the path and move quietly south of it through the trees, giving the sound a wide berth. You do not look back.

The sobbing fades. After a while there is no sound at all. Just your own breathing and the small sounds of your boots in the leaves.

The path curves back to you a half mile on. You rejoin it.

d_20.text:
You move through the trees toward the sound. You come on him in a small hollow in the lee of a fallen beech.

He is sitting with his back against the log, knees drawn up, hands over his face. He is in middle years — heavyset, weather-worn, the dark stained clothes of a charcoal-burner. There is a small bundle next to him and a billhook in the leaf-litter at his feet. His hat is off. His hair is matted with sweat.

He hears you and looks up. He does not reach for the billhook. His face is the grey of someone four days lost and badly frightened, and when he tries to speak he has to start three times before the words come.

"You're not one of them," he says. It is not a question. It is something he is making sure of.

choices.text: Sit with him. Share food and water. Let him talk.
Speak with him gently. Share what little water you have, but not food.
Tell him you cannot stay. Move on.
Keep a hand on your knife. Treat him as a threat.

d_20_help.text:
You sit on the leaf-litter across from him and unbuckle your pack. You break a portion of provisions and pass it to him. He stares at it for a long moment before he takes it. He eats slowly, in small careful bites, like someone who has remembered partway through that he is starving.

You pass him the waterskin. He drinks and gives it back. The colour is coming into his face.

He is from Vethara, he says. The charcoal-burner community at the upper edge of the valley, four days' walk south-east of here. He came in to cut hazel for the kilns and he lost the line of his own marks on the second day. Since then he has been walking in circles and drinking from streams that have made him sick. He says he has seen things he cannot account for. A stand of pine where every needle was on the ground. A fox that came up to him and would not move when he shouted. He says the word for it in his own dialect, which you do not know, and then he says: wrong. Wrong all through.

He does not know the way out any better than you do. But he can describe Vethara, and the path that leads to it from the deep forest, in the precise detail of a man who has walked it a thousand times. He makes you repeat it back to him before he is satisfied.

You give him a second portion of provisions for the road. He does not have the strength to come with you. He says he will rest and then walk south by the sun.

You leave him sitting against the log with his hat back on.

d_20_no_food.text:
You crouch in front of him with empty hands. You tell him you have no food. You tell him you have a little water, and he drinks what you can spare.

He looks at you a long time. Then he tells you what he can, in case you make it out and he does not. He is from Vethara — the charcoal-burner community at the upper edge of the valley. He came in to cut hazel and lost his marks. He has seen things he cannot account for. He thinks something is in the water. He thinks it has been put there.

He makes you repeat the path to Vethara back to him before he is satisfied.

You leave him sitting against the log. There is nothing else you can do.

d_20_leave.text:
You step back from the hollow. He watches you go. He does not call after you. He does not move.

You rejoin the path with a feeling in your chest you do not name. The sobbing does not start again.

d_20_wary.text:
You keep your hand on the hilt of your knife. He sees this. Something changes in his face — not anger. A tired closing-over.

"Suit yourself," he says.

He stands up too quickly. The billhook is in his hand. You do not think he means to use it but he is past sensible thought, four days lost and starving, and the sight of an armed stranger has snapped something in him.

He comes at you.

d_20_after_combat.text:
He goes down in the leaf-litter and does not get up. After a moment you see that he is breathing, shallow and ragged.

You crouch by him and look at his face. You do not know whether he will live. You do what you can — you set the billhook out of reach, you fold his coat under his head, you leave him your second waterskin if you have one and a portion of food if you can spare it. The wrongness of it sits in you.

You stand and walk on. You do not look back.

d_20_fled.text:
You back away with both hands raised, then turn and run. He does not pursue you. After thirty paces the trees close and you cannot hear him.

You walk for a long time before you slow.

d_22.text:
The path climbs out of the valley. The trees thin into a band of older oaks, wide-spaced, the leaf-litter deep and dry.

You walk for an hour. The light comes down at low angles through the trunks. The shadows are long. You realise, after a while, that you have not seen or heard a single bird in all of that hour. Not a wing-beat. Not a call. Not even the distant complaint of a crow.

There is movement at the edge of your vision and you turn fast. It is a dark shape low in the leaf-litter, twenty paces off — moving the wrong way for a hare, the wrong shape for a fox. You stand still and watch.

choices.text: Approach quietly to see what it is.
Leave it alone and continue walking.

d_23.text:
You move toward it slowly, keeping to soft ground. At ten paces you can see it clearly.

It is a badger. It is moving in a small tight circle in the leaves, head low, walking the same circle over and over. You watch for a long minute. The circle is perhaps three feet across. The badger walks it with absolute concentration, as if it has somewhere to be at the next step and the next.

It does not notice you. It does not stop.

You step back into the trees and leave it to its circle.

d_24.text: You leave it alone. Whatever it was is its own business. You keep walking and after a hundred paces the path bends and the movement is behind you and forgotten.

d_25.text:
Where the path drops down into a hollow you find a stand of trees you have not seen before — not in shape, but in colour.

They are beeches, eight or nine of them, growing close. The trunks at the base, where they meet the leaf-litter, carry a discolouration in a band a hand's breadth high. It is the colour of wet rust, but it is not rust. It runs around each trunk in a continuous band, even on the side furthest from the slope. It is not weather-staining. It is not lichen. You crouch and look at it for a long time.

The smell is here, stronger than at the river. Faintly chemical. Wrong for rot. Wrong for blight.

choices.text: Cut a sample of the discoloured bark with your knife.
Note the spot, take nothing, and move on.

d_25_sample.text:
You take the knife and slice a thin curl of bark from one of the trunks at the discoloured band. The bark comes away easier than it should. The cut surface beneath is wet, and the discolouration transfers immediately to the steel of the blade — a faint orange-brown smear that does not wipe clean on your sleeve.

You hold the blade up close. The smell on it is sharper than the smell in the air. It is the smell of the water at the river. It is the smell on the muzzle of the deer.

You wrap the bark sample carefully in a scrap of cloth and stow it in your pack. You wipe the blade on the leaf-litter. The stain on the steel does not come off.

d_27.text:
You climb out of the hollow and the smell thins, though it does not leave you. You realise after a while that you cannot tell whether you can still smell it or whether it has settled in your nose.

The path runs on along the contour of a long slope. You walk. The light is starting to slant.

choices.text: Push on while there is still light.
Stop and rest a while — sit, drink, breathe.
Step off the path to forage among a pocket of healthier trees.

d_28.text:
You stop in a sheltered spot in the lee of a great oak and sit with your back to the trunk. You eat a portion of provisions if you have them. You drink, sparingly, from your skin.

The forest goes on without you for a quarter hour. Nothing comes near. Nothing makes a sound that requires your attention.

When you stand again your legs are stiff but the worst of the day's tiredness has eased back.

d_30.text:
The slant of the light tells you the day is going. You estimate two hours of useful walking left.

The path forks. The left fork rises along the slope toward higher ground. The right fork drops into thicker trees, following what looks like a small dry watercourse. The map shows neither fork.

choices.text: Take the lower fork into the trees.

d_31.text:
You climb. The path along the higher ground is firmer and easier walking, and after a quarter hour it brings you out onto a long shoulder of bare rock with a view across the canopy.

The view is bad.

From up here you can see what was hidden below: a long pale streak through the green of the forest, a quarter mile wide and running roughly with the line of the watershed. From above it looks like a winter colour through summer trees — not dead, exactly. Dying. Half the canopy in that strip is gone.

You look a long time before you climb back down.

choices.text: Descend and find a place to camp.

d_32.text:
You take the lower fork. The path drops into thicker trees and the air cools. The dry watercourse runs beside you, choked with leaf-fall and old branches.

A quarter mile in the watercourse opens into a small flat hollow ringed by hornbeam, and at the centre of the hollow is the unmistakable shape of a camp.

d_33.text:
You descend from the bare rock back into the trees. The light is going faster now. You need ground for the night.

The slope evens out into a shallow draw choked with hornbeam. Through the trees ahead you see the unmistakable shape of a camp.

d_35.text:
You stop at the edge of the hollow and look in.

It is a camp. It has been a camp for some time. There is a tarp pitched between three trees with a low neat ridge, the canvas weathered but intact. Beneath the tarp a bedroll, a folded blanket, a small stack of cut firewood. A firepit, the stones blackened, the ash old. A workbench made of a split log with two stumps as supports. On the workbench: a row of small glass vials in a wooden rack, three of them empty, two of them stoppered and full of something dark. Beside the rack a notebook, weighted shut by a stone.

The camp is intact and abandoned. There is no sign of struggle. No sign of hurry. There is also no sign that anyone has been here in some days.

You know, before you cross to the workbench, before you read a word of it: this is Marek's.

choices.text: Go to the workbench. Take the journal.
Search the bedroll and the pack first.
Look at the firepit and the wood-stack.
Walk the perimeter of the camp first.

d_35a.text:
You crouch by the bedroll. It has been folded — not thrown back, not left open as a man leaving in haste would leave it. Folded in thirds, the blanket squared on top. There is a leather satchel beside it, its flap closed and buckled.

You open the satchel. Inside: a spare shirt, neatly rolled. A small whetstone. A pencil case. A leather wallet of folded papers — not the journal, but earlier work, sketches and lists in Marek's hand. Maps with watershed lines drawn in. A small purse of coins.

A man does not pack his bedroll like this if he intends to come back tonight. A man does not leave his coin purse if he intends never to return. He went somewhere he expected to be back from. He went on his own feet, and not in great hurry. And he has not come back.

choices.text: Look at the firepit and the wood-stack.
Walk the perimeter of the camp.

d_35b.text:
You crouch by the firepit. The ash is old — settled, slightly damp on the surface from a rain three or four days back. There has been no fire here for at least that long.

The wood-stack beside the pit is cut to even lengths and stacked under a small lean-to of bark to keep it dry. There is a great deal of it. Marek built up his fuel like a man who intended to be here a while.

You put your hand on the lean-to bark. It is dry. It would still light.

choices.text: Walk the perimeter of the camp.

d_35c.text:
You walk the edge of the hollow. There is one trail in — the way you came. There is, you find on the far side, one trail out — narrow, beaten into the moss by repeated walking, heading off through the trees toward the upstream of the dry watercourse. He walked it many times. To his sampling sites, you think.

At the lip of the hollow on the western side you find something else. Two sets of unfamiliar bootprints in soft ground beneath a leaning hazel. Larger than yours. Quality boots — the heel-marks crisp, not the rounded heels of working boots. Recent. Within a few days.

You look a long time at the prints before you go back to the camp.

d_36.text:
You cross to the workbench. You lift the stone off the notebook. You sit on one of the stumps with your back to the firepit and the failing light over your shoulder.

You open the notebook.

choices.text: Read it carefully, cover to cover, while there is still light.
Skim it for the most recent entries.

d_38_full.text:
You read it through. It takes you the last of the light.

The early entries are Marek as you remember him. Precise dates. Locations to within a few hundred paces. The watershed mapped and re-mapped. Sample numbers cross-referenced to the vials on the rack. The handwriting open and unhurried.

A week in, a pattern: a discolouration in the bank-soil at certain points along the river. Always on the same bank. Always at distances that do not correspond to natural deposit. He notes the regularity and is, in that early entry, only puzzled.

A few days later he has walked the watershed. The pattern holds. The discolouration follows the line of the hydrology with — he writes the word in capitals — INTENT. He names the compound by its mineral signature. He calls it chrysite. He notes it is not native to this geology. He notes it would have been carried in.

The entries shorten. The handwriting tightens.

The second-to-last entry, dated five days ago: "A surveyor's party came through the camp this morning. Two men, very polite. Imperial papers, in order. They said they were mapping the watershed for the new forestry assessment and had been asked to check in on me, as I had been in the field a long time. They offered to escort me back to the edge. I told them I needed another week. They were very understanding. They knew the precise location of my western sampling site. I had not told them. They left at noon and I have not slept tonight."

The last entry, dated three days ago, is a single line: "I am going to the upstream point in the morning. If anyone reads this who is not me — be careful at the cache."

You close the notebook. The light is gone. You sit in the dark for a long time before you move to lay your bedroll next to his.

choices.text: Sleep here. Push on at first light.

d_38_skim.text:
You flick through the notebook in the failing light, looking for the recent entries. The early pages are dense with measurements and you let them go.

The last few entries are shorter. He mentions a compound by name — chrysite — and a pattern in the watershed. He mentions a surveyor's party who came through the camp five days ago. Imperial papers. Very polite. He writes that they knew the location of his western sampling site and that he had not told them.

The last entry is a line: "I am going to the upstream point in the morning. If anyone reads this who is not me — be careful at the cache."

You close the notebook. The light is going. You will read it properly when there is time.

choices.text: Sleep here. Push on at first light.

d_40.text:
You sleep poorly. The wind moves in the canvas of the tarp. Something — once, deep in the night — calls a long single note from somewhere upstream. Not a wolf. Not an owl. You do not place it.

You wake before the light. You eat a small portion of provisions if you have them. You break camp slowly, packing Marek's notebook into the inner pocket of your coat. You leave the tarp and the bedroll. He may yet come back.

You stand at the edge of the hollow. The narrow trail leading upstream — Marek's working trail — is faint in the half-light. You take it.

d_42.text:
The trail follows the dry watercourse upstream into thicker trees. The going is slower than yesterday. You stop frequently to find the next mark.

The forest here is in worse shape. The canopy gap is wider, and the standing dead wood is more frequent — pale grey trunks rising out of the leaf-litter, branchless, the bark sloughed away. The smell is in the air constantly now, faint but constant, and the back of your tongue is sore with it.

The map you carry is no help. The dry watercourse is not on it. The contour of the land does not match. You navigate by Marek's marks alone — small notches cut into trunks at eye height, every fifty paces or so. Without them you would be lost in an hour.

The trail forks. One branch continues upstream along the watercourse. The other turns north, climbing out of the valley toward higher ground. Both are marked.

choices.text: Continue upstream along the watercourse — toward Marek's sampling sites.
Take the north branch — toward higher ground.

d_43.text:
You stop in a small clearing where the trail crosses a patch of sun. You drink. You check the knife at your belt. You look back the way you came and see only the trees closing the trail behind you.

There is a sound, brief and small, behind you in the leaf-litter. You turn fast. There is nothing.

test_luck.lucky_text: You stand a long minute. Whatever it was does not repeat. Probably a settled branch, a movement of pressure in the wood. You turn forward and go on.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Out of the corner of your eye, fifty paces back along the trail, you catch a movement that is not the movement of trees. It is gone before you can resolve it. You stand a long minute waiting for it to come again. It does not. You go on, but slower, and you stop more often to look behind you.

d_44.text:
You follow the watercourse to its highest reach — a cleft in a low ridge where in wetter seasons a small spring would feed it. The spring is not running. The cleft is dry.

This is where Marek's work was. You can see the marks of it: small wooden pegs driven into the bank at intervals, each numbered. A sequence of five, twenty paces apart, all on the eastern bank. Where the pegs are, the soil is visibly wrong — a paler colour, a finer texture, with the distinct absence of the small herbs and mosses that grow elsewhere in the cleft. A dead band, two feet wide, running with the line of the watercourse.

You kneel by the second peg. Beside it, almost hidden in the leaves, you find what you were hoping for: a small cloth roll of empty glass vials. Spares. Marek's hand.

choices.text: Take samples carefully with the knife — proper cuts, sealed vials.
Take what samples you can with your bare hands.

d_44a.text:
You work slowly. You take a clean vial, scrape soil into it with the flat of the knife from a point clear of the peg, stopper it, mark the position by the peg number on a scrap of cloth. You do this five times. The smell as you cut into the soil is unmistakable: the river-smell, sharper.

The stained band is consistent at every peg. The depth of the discolouration is the same. The pattern is not what a flood deposit looks like. It is not what a leak from a single source looks like. It is what something looks like when it has been put, deliberately, at intervals along a watershed.

You stow the vials carefully in the centre of your pack, wrapped in cloth. Your hands smell of it for the rest of the day no matter how many times you wipe them on the leaves.

choices.text: Leave the cleft and find a place to think.

d_44b.text:
You scoop soil into the vials with your bare hands. The work is dirty and you cannot keep it clean. The smell is on you for the rest of the day. The samples are imperfect, but they are samples.

The stained band is consistent at every peg. The pattern is not natural. It has been put here.

choices.text: Leave the cleft and find a place to think.

d_46.text:
You sit on the ridge above the cleft. The morning is cold. You hold the vials in their cloth wrap on your lap and look down at the dead band running along the watercourse below.

You think about Marek's last entry. The upstream point. He was here, three or four days ago, doing what you have just done.

He did not come back to the camp.

choices.text: Continue. There is more to find.

d_47.text:
You take the north branch. The trail climbs steeply out of the valley through dying birch. You stop twice to breathe.

The trail levels onto a low ridge and bends west, and then you see it ahead through the trees: a small, neatly built shelter against a stone outcrop. The roof is sound. There is no smoke from the chimney-vent. Beside the door, an axe is leaned against the wall — the steel free of rust, the haft oiled. Recently used.

This is not Marek's. The build is wrong, the materials too uniform — milled timber, brought in.

choices.text: Watch from the trees a while before approaching.
Leave it alone and go back down to the watercourse.

d_47a.text:
You sit in the trees forty paces back and watch.

A quarter hour. Nothing moves at the shelter. The axe stays where it is. No smoke. No sound. There is nobody home — or nobody home at this moment.

You wait a little longer. The stillness is complete.

choices.text: Leave it. Go back down to the watercourse.

d_47_back.text: You back away from the ridge and pick your way down to the watercourse. The descent is harder than the climb. You do not look back at the shelter.

d_48.text:
You cross to the shelter. The door is unlatched. Inside, it is dry and ordered.

A narrow bunk along one wall, made up with a clean blanket. A small iron stove with kindling stacked beside it. A shelf of provisions — hard biscuit, cured meat in waxed paper, a sealed jar of honey. A flask of water on a low table, the metal bright. Folded clothes on a chest. A spare oil-lamp.

It is the kindest sight you have seen in two days. It is also too kind. The stove is brushed clean. The blanket is recently aired. Someone passed through here within the last few days and left it expecting to use it again — or expecting someone to find it.

choices.text: Examine the shelter carefully before touching anything — look at maker's marks.
Examine the shelter carefully — Marek warned you about a cache.
Eat, drink, and rest. You need this.
Take nothing. Leave the way you came.

d_48a.text:
You move slowly through the shelter, looking for marks.

The stove has a maker's stamp on the underside: a small five-pointed star inside a circle. The waxed paper of the cured meat carries the same mark, faintly, in dark red ink. The blanket — when you turn back its corner — has the mark embroidered into a hem-tag in dark thread.

You know the mark. It is the seal of the imperial supply. You have seen it before on requisition orders for the marches. You see it now in a charcoal-burner's shelter four days deep into a forest no imperial supply train has any business in.

This is not a cache for travellers. This is a cache that has been put here, for someone, by people who do not want their hand seen. The food is probably safe. Probably.

You stand a long time in the doorway with the choice in front of you.

choices.text: Take what you need. You are starving and you cannot afford pride.
Take nothing. Whatever this place is, you will not be in their debt.

d_48_use.text:
