You eat. The biscuit is dry but good. The cured meat tastes of salt and smoke and — if you let yourself notice — nothing else, which is the taste of food that has been properly preserved by people with money and equipment. You drink from the flask. The water is clean and slightly metallic.

You rest a half hour on the bunk. Your legs stop trembling. The light through the small window is clear.

When you stand to leave you fold the blanket as you found it. You take a portion of the biscuit and meat for the road. You leave the door as you found it.

d_48_leave.text:
You step back from the shelter and pull the door to. You do not eat from it. You do not drink from it. You do not take anything.

Whatever else you have lost in this forest, you have not yet sat down at someone else's table without knowing whose table it is.

You turn down the slope toward the watercourse with your stomach tight and your hands cold.

d_50.text:
You walk on for an hour. The forest is the forest, bad in the ways you have come to expect. You climb a low ridge and stop on the open ground at the top, and you sit down in the leaf-litter with your back against a fallen trunk and you let what you know assemble itself.

Marek's notes. The dead band along the watercourse. The cleft above. The pegs at intervals. The chrysite he named in his hand. The smell on the deer's muzzle, the discolouration on the bark, the cast in the colour of the river. The badger walking its small circle. The deer that lay down without a wound. The empty rotting log.

You think also of the surveyors. The ones who said they were mapping the watershed. Who knew the location of Marek's western sampling site without being told. Who left at noon, very polite, very understanding.

It is not a forest dying.

It is a forest being killed. By a hand. With a purpose.

choices.text: Stand. Walk on. There is more to do.

d_52.text:
You walk the ridge along its length. The trail has all but disappeared. You navigate by sun and slope, working broadly westward by your sense of where the edge of the forest must be.

The afternoon is warm and there is no wind. The smell sits on the ground.

choices.text: Press on through the warm afternoon.
Stop and rest in the shade.

d_53.text:
You rest in the shade of a great beech that is not yet sick. The bark is sound. There is moss on the north side. You put your hand against the trunk and you feel the small chill of a tree that is doing its work.

You stay longer than you mean to. When you stand again the light has shifted considerably.

d_54.text:
The ridge drops away into a long descent through mixed wood. The path you come down is not a path so much as a line of least resistance — but at the foot of the slope you find a beaten line in the leaves that is unmistakably a trail. Not Marek's. Wider. Walked by more than one person.

You crouch and look at it. Two sets of bootprints, perhaps three. The same crisp heel-marks you saw at the camp.

choices.text: Follow the trail at a careful distance.
Cross the trail and continue west off the line.

d_55.text:
You follow the trail at a distance, keeping to softer ground where you will leave less sign. The trail runs west along the contour, then bends and drops into a stand of close-grown pine.

In the pine you stop, because the trail is no longer the only thing on it.

A wolf is standing on the trail twenty paces ahead. It is thin. Its coat is patchy. It is in the wrong country for the season — wolves should be much higher this time of year, in the open country above the treeline. It is not crouched. It is not ready to come at you. It is standing in the trail with its head low and its ears flat and looking at you with a look that is not predator and not prey. It is the look of an animal as lost as you are.

choices.text: Light a branch from the flint and steel and drive it off.
Light a torch with your flint and steel — fire will scare it.
Back away slowly without breaking eye contact.
Stand your ground. Knife in hand.

d_55_fire.text:
You crouch slowly, keeping your eyes on it, and gather a handful of dry needles and a strip of bark from the nearest pine. You strike the flint twice before the spark takes. The flame catches small and then runs up the bark.

You stand and step forward with the burning bark held at arm's length. The wolf retreats two paces. You step forward again, and again. It turns and goes off the trail at an unhurried angle and disappears among the pines.

You stand a minute holding the burning bark. You let it burn down on a patch of bare earth and you grind the last of it out with your boot. The smell of pine smoke hangs in the cold air and is, briefly, the cleanest thing you have smelled all day.

d_55_back.text: You take a step back. Slow. Then another. You keep your eyes on the wolf and your hands open at your sides.

test_luck.lucky_text: It watches you for a long moment. Then it turns its head away — a small dismissive motion, like a creature that has decided you are not its problem — and it walks off the trail into the pines and is gone. You stand a minute breathing before you walk on.
test_luck.unlucky_text: It does not move. You take another step back. Something in the way you move — too fast, too eager to be away — changes its mind for it. It comes forward at a low quick lope and there is no more time.

d_55_combat.text: You set your feet. You bring the knife up. The wolf reads your stance and decides.

d_55_won.text:
It dies hard. When it is over you are kneeling beside it in the pine needles with your hands shaking and the knife red and the smell of pine sap and blood mixed in your nose.

It was thin. The ribs are visible through the patchy coat. There was almost nothing in its belly.

You clean the knife on a tuft of needles and stand.

d_55_fled.text:
You break off the line and run. The wolf does not pursue you for long — perhaps fifty paces — and then it stops, and after another fifty paces of running you stop too, with your hands on your knees and your chest burning.

You walk a wide loop through the pines and rejoin the trail a quarter mile further on. You do not see the wolf again.

d_56.text:
You step over the trail and continue west off the line of it. The going is harder — no path, more deadfall, the slope working against you — but you are not on a trail that someone else has walked.

A half mile of this and you are tired and scratched and the light is going. You climb a small rise and from the top you can see, through the trees, a thin column of woodsmoke a quarter mile to the south.

choices.text: Avoid the smoke. Camp rough out here.

d_56_camp.text:
You make a cold camp in the lee of a fallen oak. No fire — the smoke would bring whoever made the other smoke to you. You eat a portion of provisions if you have them. You sleep in your coat.

The night is long. You wake twice to nothing. In the morning the cold has settled into your bones and you are slower starting than you would like to be.

choices.text: Rise and continue west toward the forest edge.

d_57.text:
You walk on along the trail another half hour. The pines thin and the ground levels and you smell, before you see, a fire.

Woodsmoke. Clean — birch and beech, properly seasoned. Coming from somewhere ahead and to the south of the trail.

choices.text: Step off the trail and skirt wide around the smoke.

d_58.text:
You move toward the smoke from the upwind side and crouch in the trees forty paces back.

It is a small neat camp in a clearing. Two men. A cookfire. A canvas wall-tent of good quality. Two packs leaned against a log, military in their cut. The men are not in uniform. They are dressed for the country — good wool, sound boots — and they are eating from tin plates and talking quietly.

They are the surveyors. The same two from the description in Marek's journal. You have not seen them before but you know them at sight.

One of them looks up. He looks directly at the patch of trees where you are crouched and he raises a hand in unhurried greeting. He calls out, in an easy voice: "Walker. We've been hoping you'd come this way. Come and have something hot."

He names a campsite — a small place by a feeder stream where you slept the night you crossed the river margin. You did not tell him about it. You did not tell anyone.

choices.text: Stand up. Walk into the camp.
Stay where you are. Watch a moment longer.

d_58_watch.text:
You stay crouched. The man who called out lowers his hand. He says something to his companion, low, and they go on with their meal as if you were not there. They do not come looking. They do not get up.

This, somehow, is worse than if they had.

The minutes pass. The light is going. You will have to do something soon — sit out here all night with no fire, or walk in to them, or leave them and go on.

choices.text: Stand up. Walk into the camp.
Withdraw quietly. Find your own way west in the dusk.

d_58_approach.text:
You stand up out of the trees. They watch you walk in without surprise. The one who called out — older, broader, a face that smiles easily — gets up and offers you a tin plate. The other does not stand. He nods once and goes on eating.

You take the plate. Stew. Properly seasoned. The first hot food you have had in two days.

"You'll be looking for Marek," the older one says. He says it kindly. "He's safe. We found him three days ago, came down with a fever from drinking the bad water — he'd been pushing himself too hard. We've got him resting at our forward camp, half a day west of here. We're heading back at first light. You're welcome to walk with us."

He smiles. The smile is the smile of a man who knows you have nowhere else to go.

You look at the stew on your plate. You look at the second man, who has not stopped eating. You look at the trees behind them, which give nothing away.

choices.text: Accept. Eat, sleep here, walk with them at first light.
Thank them. Eat. Then make excuses and go on alone in the dusk.

d_58_go_with.text:
You eat the stew. You sleep by their fire. They are courteous. They do not ask you questions. They do not search your pack while you sleep, as far as you can tell — and you check it carefully in the morning, and nothing is missing, and that is somehow not a comfort.

In the grey of first light you break camp with them and you walk west at their pace. The older one tells you stories about the survey work, easy stories, well told. The younger one walks behind you. You feel his eyes on the back of your neck the whole way.

You are walking, you understand quite clearly, into something. You have decided, for reasons you would rather not examine, to walk into it on your own feet.

d_58_decline.text:
You finish the stew. You hand back the plate. You tell them, with as much grace as you can manage, that you have agreed to meet a colleague at a marked point further west and that you will press on while there is light.

The older one nods slowly. "Suit yourself," he says. "Mind the wolves. There's a pack come down out of the high country."

You nod. You shoulder your pack. You walk out of the firelight and into the dusk.

The trees close behind you. You walk hard for a quarter hour before you slow.

choices.text: Push on west in the failing light.

d_58_navigate.text: The light is bad. The ground is unfamiliar. You navigate by what slope you can feel under your boots and by the sense, more than the sight, of the slope of the ridge ahead.

test_luck.lucky_text: You find a clear line under a stand of beech that takes you up and over the low ridge with no real trouble. On the far side you make a cold camp in the deep leaf-litter and you sleep in your coat. The night is long but uneventful.
test_luck.unlucky_text: You take a wrong line down a slope that turns into a long crashing slide through brambles. You come to rest at the bottom scratched and winded, with one ankle that does not feel right when you put weight on it. You make a cold camp where you lie. You will pay for this in the morning.

d_27_aside.text:
You step off the path to the side and find a small pocket of older trees the contamination seems to have spared. The bark is sound. There is moss. Beneath one beech, in the leaf-litter, you find a cluster of late autumn russula — the brown-capped kind, edible if you trust the look of them.

You crouch and examine them. They look right. They smell right. You take three and stow them in a cloth pocket of the pack.

h_1.text:
You hear him before you see him.

Marek's voice, threading through the alders down a slope you have not yet crested. The cadence is wrong. He is answering, not speaking. Three short replies in the time it takes you to step over a rotten birch. A pause. Another short reply.

You go flat against the trunk and listen. The second voice is unhurried. Calm in a way that does not belong to anyone lost in this forest. You move forward in a crouch, one boot then the other, until the camp opens below you through a screen of bracken.

It is well-pitched. Two oilcloth tents, set square to the wind. A fire built down to good embers, not the smoking heap of the desperate. Three figures. One stands at the tree line with a rifle across his forearms, watching the wrong way — east, away from you. One squats by a packframe, sorting straps. The third sits on a folding stool by the fire, and that one is talking to Marek, and Marek is wrapped in a grey blanket with his hands around a tin cup, looking at the embers.

He looks smaller than you remember. Not thinner. Smaller. Drawn down inside himself.

The man on the stool says something you cannot catch. Marek nods. Marek says, yes. The man on the stool nods back, slowly, as though confirming something already understood between them.

choices.text: Walk down into the camp openly. He's alive — that is what you came for.

h_2.text:
You stand up out of the bracken and the man with the rifle turns before your boot has completed its first step. He does not raise the weapon. He simply stops what he was doing and watches you come, and the fact that he was not surprised is the first thing you understand about him.

The man on the stool stands. He is older than the others, perhaps fifty, clean-shaven, with the kind of weathered face that comes from outdoor work conducted without urgency. He smiles when he sees you. The smile reaches his eyes. He looks like a man who has been waiting for a guest and is pleased the guest has arrived.

Marek looks up from his cup. His face does something complicated and then settles, and he says your name, quietly, and adds, you should not have come.

h_3.text:
You stay low. The wind is in your face, which is luck — they will not catch your scent, and any sound carries away from you toward the ridge.

You watch for a count of two hundred. Long enough to learn the camp's grammar.

The rifleman is not patrolling. He is anchored, eyes on the eastern approach, the direction a man limping out of the deeper forest would most likely come. He is waiting for someone. Not for you, because he is not watching the slope you came down. For someone else.

The one with the packframe finishes sorting straps and goes to the second tent and lifts the flap and speaks to whoever is inside, and the answering voice is short and crisp and carries the cadence of a man giving orders.

The older man by the fire never stops watching Marek. Even while he speaks, even while he sips from his own tin cup, his attention sits on your mentor like a hand resting on a shoulder.

choices.text: Step out and walk down. You have seen what you needed to see — that he is alive.
Pull back. Circle the camp. There may be a better way to reach him.

h_4.text:
You stand and the rifleman sees you in the same heartbeat. He does not raise the rifle. He calls something soft over his shoulder, and the older man at the fire looks up and finds you on the slope, and his face arranges itself into pleasure.

You go down.

h_5.text: You back out the way you came, slow and low, until the camp is below the rise and you can stand without being seen. You circle north, working through the thinning birches, looking for a vantage from which Marek might see you without the others.

h_6.text:
His face goes through three things at once. Recognition. Relief. And then something tightening — the calculation of a man who has just had a problem made larger.

He glances back at the camp. Then at you.

Go, he says, very quietly. Not back the way you came. North. Stay off the markers. There is a forester's hut northeast of here, half a mile, under the hearthstone — 

A voice from the camp calls his name. Friendly. Patient.

Get out, he says. I will find a way. Then, as though continuing a thought aloud: under the hearthstone. Northeast.

He picks up the kettle and turns and walks back up the slope. He does not look back.

choices.text: Honour what he asked. Move northeast toward the hut.
You cannot leave him there. Walk into the camp.

h_10.text:
The older man comes around the fire to meet you. He extends a hand. His grip is dry and exactly firm enough.

Serrin, he says. Imperial Survey, Northern Marches. You must be the friend.

He says it as though Marek has spoken of you often. Perhaps he has. Marek is still sitting on the stool, the blanket over his shoulders, the cup in his hands, looking up at you with an expression that does not quite resolve.

We found him three days ago, Serrin says, gesturing for you to sit. He had turned his ankle and run his supplies to the bottom. Lost in his own woods, can you imagine. He smiles at Marek, fond. Marek manages a small upturn of the mouth in return.

You are welcome to share what we have, Serrin says. Tea. There is a stew on shortly. We are walking out tomorrow, all of us together, and I assume you will want to walk out with us.

The rifleman has gone back to watching the eastern approach. The man at the packframe glances at you once, mildly, and returns to his work. The fire is good. The tea, when Serrin pours it, smells of bergamot and something else, herbal, that you cannot place.

h_11.text:
You take the cup. Serrin nods, pleased, and settles back onto his stool. The tea is warm. You do not drink it immediately. Marek watches you over the rim of his own.

Serrin makes conversation. He is good at it. He asks where you came in, and listens to your answer with genuine attention, and corrects himself once when he names a tributary wrong and laughs at his own mistake. He is the kind of man you would want to drink with, in a different season, in a different forest.

You notice, after some minutes, that no one has asked what you were doing here.

choices.text: Wait for an opening to speak with Marek alone.

h_12.text:
Thank you, you say, but you have water of your own, and you would prefer to stand for a moment, your legs are stiff.

Serrin smiles as though this were the most reasonable thing anyone has said to him in a week. Of course, he says. Stretch. Walk. The camp is yours.

He does not seem put out. He resumes his conversation with the man at the packframe — quiet, technical, something about a strap. Marek watches you from his stool. He does not speak. He does not need to. The not-speaking is itself a communication.

choices.text: Move closer to Marek without making it obvious.

h_20.text:
The chance comes. Serrin is bent over a folding map with the packframe man, both of them facing away. The rifleman is still looking east. Marek rises slowly, the blanket sliding from his shoulders, and goes to the kettle, and you find a reason to be near the kettle too.

Three sentences. He gives you three sentences, in a voice barely above the sound of his own breathing, while pretending to test the kettle's weight.

h_21.text:
You read it, he says. Good. The documents are under the hearthstone of the old forester's hut. Northeast, half a mile. A leather case. Take it out before anything else happens.

He puts the kettle down. He does not look at you again. He carries the kettle back toward the fire and Serrin glances up and smiles and says something about the tea being almost stewed.

choices.text: Sit with what you have just been told.

h_22.text:
Don't drink the tea, he says. Don't trust Serrin. There are documents — I cannot tell you where yet, not here. Stay close, find me alone again.

He puts the kettle down. He does not look at you again. He carries the kettle back toward the fire and Serrin glances up and smiles and says something about the tea being almost stewed.

choices.text: Sit with what you have just been told.

h_25.text:
You move back toward the fire because moving away from it now would be the wrong shape. Serrin pours you more tea you have not drunk. He talks. The packframe man laughs at something Serrin says. The rifleman remains at his post.

The question is not what Marek told you. The question is whether he believes you are here because you came alone, or because Serrin's people brought you in.

choices.text: He needs to know you came on your own. (You did not trust the survey team.)
He saw you arrive friendly with their kind. Show him his own journal. (Have journal.)
He saw you arrive friendly with their kind. You have nothing of his. Risk it. (Test your luck.)

h_26.text:
Later, as the light goes blue and Serrin sets a second pot to boil, Marek finds another moment. He does not need much. A glance, held a quarter-second too long. A nod.

It is enough. He knows you are not theirs. He knows you came in alone, off the markers, off the cache route, and that everything you say from this point can be trusted as your own.

He is not free. He is cooperating with them because the alternative was worse, in some way you do not yet understand. But he is on your side of the line, and he believes you are on his.

h_27.text:
Later, as Serrin steps away to relieve himself in the trees, you cross to the fire and crouch beside Marek as if to warm your hands. You let the corner of the journal show inside your jacket. Just enough.

His eyes find it. He understands. Not given to you by Serrin's people. Found in the cabin, where you knew to look. You came on his trail, not on theirs.

He looks back at the fire. He says, quietly, without moving his lips much: alright. Alright.

h_28.text: You have nothing to show him. You have only your face, your presence, the fact of having walked all this way. You will have to make him believe you on those terms alone.

test_luck.lucky_text: He watches you across the fire while Serrin is talking. You hold his gaze a moment longer than is comfortable. Something passes between you. He inclines his head a fraction, and you understand that you have been believed.
test_luck.unlucky_text: He watches you across the fire while Serrin is talking, and what you see in his face is a man who would like to trust you and cannot afford to. He looks away first. The window does not open.

h_29.text: You have him. Whatever happens next, he will move with you, not against you.

h_30.text: You will have to do this without him. He will not actively betray you — that is not who he is — but he will not risk what he believes is the only hand he still has to play. Whatever is in front of you, you carry it alone.

h_35.text:
Serrin finds you, after a time, on the far side of the camp where you have gone to look at nothing in particular. He arrives without sound. He stands beside you, hands in his coat pockets, looking out into the dying birches with an expression of mild regret.

It has been a difficult few days for everyone, he says. Your friend, especially. He has been in the field too long. He is tired. He has — and Serrin pauses here, choosing the word with care — formed some impressions. Of the work. Of us. Impressions a tired man forms when he has been alone too long with his own measurements.

The simplest thing, he says, is for all of us to walk out of here together tomorrow. The survey is essentially complete. Your friend goes home. You go home. The file closes, missing-person to recovered, no further notation. We have done this kind of thing before. It is, he says, the kindest outcome for everyone.

He does not mention the documents. He does not need to.

h_36.text:
You know what he is asking you to walk past. You have stood at the edge of the dieback. You have seen the rings of brown moss radiating out from sources that did not exist before someone put something in the soil. The kindest outcome for everyone is also the outcome in which no one ever explains what is killing this forest.

Serrin watches your face. He is patient. He is, in fact, sympathetic — that is the worst thing about him. He believes the offer he is making is a generous one, and within the frame he has chosen, it is.

choices.text: Agree, while you think of something else.
Tell him you need to walk a moment. Stall.

h_37.text:
Alright, you say. Tomorrow.

Serrin nods, as though you have together resolved a small administrative matter. He claps you lightly on the shoulder. Good, he says. Good. He returns to the fire.

That night you sleep in the spare tent, and you do not sleep well.

h_38.text:
I need to walk, you say. I have not stopped walking in five days and I cannot stop now. Give me an hour.

Of course, Serrin says. Of course. The light is good for another hour. Do not go far.

You step out of the camp. As soon as the bracken is between you and the rifleman's line of sight, you stop pretending to walk and start moving with purpose.

h_39.text:
I do not think I will be walking out with you, you say. Not tomorrow. Not the way you mean.

Serrin looks at you for a long moment. He is not angry. He is recalibrating. You can see it happen behind his eyes — the closing of one set of options, the opening of another, the small administrative weariness of a man who has just been required to do a more difficult version of his job.

Alright, he says, finally. Alright. Take a walk. Think it over. The offer stands until morning.

The rifleman has turned, very slightly, and is now watching you.

choices.text: Walk out of the camp while you still can.

h_45.text:
You go north. Not far — far enough that the camp is no longer in sight, no longer in earshot. You stop at the foot of an old beech and put your back against it and breathe.

What Marek said. What Serrin offered. The two together build a shape you do not want to look at directly.

The forester's hut. Northeast, half a mile. He told you, or he tried to. Whether or not you believed him at the time, you believe him now.

h_48.text:
You find it where he said it would be. A low stone hut, single room, roof half-collapsed, the kind of structure built fifty years ago for a forester who has been dead forty. The door is not locked because there is no longer a door. You step over the threshold.

The hearth is cold. The hearthstone is a single slab of granite, hand-cut, set flush with a packed earth floor. You kneel and work your fingers under the lip of it. It comes up grudgingly, dragging soil with it.

Underneath: a rectangular cavity lined with oilcloth, and inside the oilcloth a leather document case bound with a waxed cord. The wax bears an imperial seal. Unbroken.

You cut the cord with your knife. Inside: papers. A great many papers. Internal correspondence on Survey letterhead. Operational timelines. Deployment records. The compound named by its military designation. The objective, on the third page, stated in a single sentence, in the dry register of a man writing for superiors who already know what they are reading.

Population clearance by resource denial.

You read it twice.

h_50.text: You roll the papers tight, slide them inside your jacket against your ribs, and leave the hut. The light is going. The forester's track points west toward the river and east toward the camp. You take neither. You go north, into ground neither set of boots has yet flattened.

h_55.text:
You read further. You sit on the edge of the hearth with the case open across your knees and you read every page.

It is not what you thought, even after Marek's notes, even after the soil samples. It is not a weapons test. The compound is the method, not the subject. The forest dying is not a side effect of the work. The forest dying is the work. The border communities — Vethara, the charcoal-burners' settlements, the half-dozen hamlets along the upper tributaries — were always going to be absorbed into the imperial frontier. The question, for the men whose names sit at the top of these letters, was only how. This way is faster. This way is quieter. There is no army to feed, no garrison to billet, no village burnt that anyone will be able to point to in fifty years and say, here, this is where it happened.

There will be nothing to point to. Just an empty forest, and the people who used to live in it dispersed to lowland labour markets, and no one alive who remembers why.

You close the case. Your hands are not steady.

choices.text: Look at the personal correspondence at the bottom of the case.

h_60.text:
Vethara is half a day off your line of escape, north and west, on the upper bend of the river. You have been there once, years ago. You remember the bridge, the long single street, the bell-house at the upper end where the road climbs into the pass.

You cover the ground hard. Your legs are not your own by the time you see the smoke of the bell-house chimney through the trees. You come down the road into the village from above and the first woman you meet on the street is hanging washing and she straightens at the sight of you and her face tells you that whatever you look like, you do not look well.

h_61.text:
You tell her, in short hard sentences, what is in the river. What is in the soil. What the men in the survey camps are doing. You tell her about the documents inside your jacket. You ask for the headman.

She does not waste time disbelieving you. She has watched the deer thin and the moss brown and the children's coughs deepen for two seasons. She had no name for it. You have given her one.

The headman is a stooped man of sixty with very clear eyes. He listens. He asks two questions and you answer both. He says, we will take the upper pasture. The bell will ring tonight. The women and the children will be on the road north before dark. Tell whoever is waiting for those papers that Vethara was warned.

He grips your forearm once, briefly. Then he turns and starts walking, and the woman with the washing is already moving toward the bell-house, and you have done what you came to do.

h_62.text:
You sit on the bench outside the bell-house and drink the water she brings and you cannot, in the end, find the words. The shape of it is too large. You tell her there is bad water in the lower river. You tell her to keep the children off the moss. She nods. She does not understand the size of what you are saying, and you cannot make her understand without staying, and you cannot stay.

You rise. You thank her. You go.

h_65.text:
You walk back into the camp from the north and Serrin is standing at the edge of the firelight as though he has been expecting you, because he has.

You tell him what you read. You name the compound. You name the operation. You watch his face for the small adjustments that mean the thing has landed.

He does not deny it. He does not, in any way, become less polite. He gestures for you to sit. He says, alright. Alright. You should hear it from someone who can explain it properly, since you have come this far.

He explains. He does it without malice, without theatricality. The empire's logic is, in his telling, coherent. The border communities were always going to be absorbed; the question was only the method. This way costs no soldiers. This way produces no martyrs. In fifty years there will be a forest growing back over good farmland and a frontier two hundred miles further north and no one will remember what was here. He says all of this in the tone of a man explaining a bridge collapse to an insurance assessor.

When he finishes, he asks, mildly, whether you have anything in your jacket that should not leave this camp.

h_66.text:
You push. You say the word out loud — clearance — and you watch his face do the only thing his face does, which is the smallest possible adjustment, and behind him the rifleman has shifted his weight onto his front foot.

This was the moment to leave. You have made the moment something else.

test_luck.lucky_text: You move first. You are out of the firelight before the rifleman has decided whether his orders permit him to act on his own initiative, and behind you Serrin is calling something steady and reasonable into the dark, and you do not stop to listen.
test_luck.unlucky_text: You do not move first. The rifleman does. The shot is not loud — the forest swallows sound — but it is enough. You are on the ground before you have understood that you have been hit, and the last thing you see is Serrin walking unhurriedly into the firelight, kneeling, opening your jacket, and removing the case with the care of a man who has done this before.

h_67.text:
Nothing, you say. Nothing in my jacket. I came to find Marek. I have found him. I will walk out tomorrow.

Serrin watches you for a long moment. Then he nods, once, slowly. Of course, he says. Of course you will.

You turn and walk to the spare tent. You do not look back. You lie down in your boots with the documents against your ribs.

h_68.text:
You meet Marek at the rendezvous, or you do not.

If he trusts you — if the moment by the kettle landed, or if the journal in your hand was enough — he will be there. He will have slipped the camp under the cover of fetching water or relieving himself in the trees, and he will have brought what he could carry, and he will know a route.

If he does not trust you, he is still in the camp, sitting by Serrin's fire, doing what he has been doing for three days, which is whatever keeps him alive the longest.

Either way, you are the one carrying out what you carry out.

h_68b.text: From the rendezvous you have a clean line of sight back across to the camp. The fire is still up. No one has yet noticed any absence. You will have, you estimate, an hour before they do.

choices.text: He's with you. The old logging track.

h_69.text:
He is at the rendezvous. He has Serrin's compass — taken when no one was looking — and a satchel with three days of hard rations and the small pistol he owned in his Survey years and never told anyone about.

The old logging track, he says. They mapped the river crossings and the markers and the high routes. They did not map the timber roads from before the consolidation. There is one west of here that runs to the ford below Vethara. We will not be on any line they are watching.

He says we. The plural is the first thing he has given you that feels entirely his own.

h_70.text:
He is not at the rendezvous. He was never coming to the rendezvous.

You stand alone at the edge of a clearing in the dark and listen for boots on bracken, and the only sound is a barn owl somewhere east of you, and after a while you understand that you are going to have to do this by yourself.

h_72.text:
Out is north and then west, around the long shoulder of the ridge, down through the dieback to where the ground is still living, and from there to the upper ford and the road beyond. The ground is bad. The light is going. You are not the only thing moving in this forest tonight.

There are choices in how you go.

choices.text: Take the river crossing — the rope makes the ford manageable.
Light a signal fire on the high ground and pray for a friendly road party.
The old logging track. Marek's route.
The hard way — overland, through the dieback, on legs and luck.

h_73.text: You reach the ford in full dark. The river is high — not flooded, but moving with intent. You secure the rope to the alder on the near bank and wade the line out, hand over hand, the cold knifing into your boots and up your calves. Halfway across the current takes your footing and the rope takes your weight and you finish on your hands and knees on the far bank, soaked through, breathing in long ragged pulls. But you are across. The agents will not bring their packs across a ford in the dark.

h_74.text:
You climb to the spur above the high ground and you build the fire there, fast, in the way the first night taught you. The flame catches. The smoke rises into the night air, marking the ridge.

It is a gamble. The right party sees it and you are out of the forest by morning. The wrong party sees it and you are not.

test_luck.lucky_text: Boots come up the ridge from the west, and they are not Serrin's. A road party from the timber company, drawn by a fire that should not have been there. They take you down to a wagon and a cup of something hot and a question you do not yet have to answer.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The boots that come up the ridge are Serrin's man at the head of them. He does not say anything. He does not need to.

h_75.text: The old logging track is exactly where Marek said it would be. Overgrown. Unmapped on any imperial chart less than thirty years old. You move down it through the long dark and Marek goes ahead, the compass dim in his hand, and neither of you speaks more than is necessary. By the grey hour before dawn you are at the upper ford. By full sun you are on the road. No one has followed.

h_76.text: You go overland. No rope, no fire, no track. The dieback is worse on the western shoulder than anywhere you have walked, and the ground gives in places where it should hold. You twist your ankle in a moss pocket and right yourself against a dead birch and keep walking. You walk most of the night.

test_luck.lucky_text: By the grey hour you are off the dieback and onto living ground, and a tributary of the upper river is loud in the dark beneath you, and you understand that you have made it.
test_luck.unlucky_text: By the grey hour you are still in the dieback, and your ankle has gone, and the stars are wrong above you because cloud has come in, and you sit down at the foot of a dead beech to rest a moment.

h_77.text:
Serrin's man is professional. He takes the case from inside your jacket. He pats you down for the rest. He does not strike you. He walks you down off the ridge to a place where Serrin is waiting, and Serrin shakes his head, very slightly, with something almost like regret.

You should have walked out with us, he says.

h_80.text:
You are out of the forest.

The road is real under your boots. There is a wagon track and the smell of woodsmoke that comes from a settled chimney rather than a hasty fire. You have walked the last hour without thinking about your feet.

What you are carrying — what you are not carrying — what is at your shoulder, or is not — these things will determine the shape of the days that follow.

h_81.text: You take stock. The documents. The man beside you, or the empty road beside you. The village upstream, warned or unwarned. The choice you made in the camp, or that the camp made of you.

h_82.text: You have the case against your ribs. The seal is broken. The papers are real.

h_83.text: He is with you on the road. He is limping, and he is not speaking, and he is alive.

h_84.text: You came in alone. You walked off no one's markers. The line you have drawn through this forest is yours.

h_90.text: You have no documents. What you have is what you remember, and memory is not what stops a thing like this.

choices.text: (you took their cache and walked their markers)

h_91.text: You came out the way they wanted you to come out. Their cache fed you. Their markers led you. Their senior man walked the last mile of road beside you and clapped you on the shoulder before he turned south.

h_92.text: Their food in your stomach. Their water in your skin.

end_exposure_approach.text:
It takes weeks. The documents go to a man in the capital who used to teach with Marek and who, after reading the first three pages, does not sleep for two days. They go from him to a journalist who has been holding a similar story together with twine and rumour for eighteen months. They go from her to a senate committee that did not want them and cannot now refuse them.

The operation does not end cleanly. These things never do. The compound is renamed, the programme is renamed, two of the men whose signatures appear in the case retire to private estates and are not pursued. But Vethara stands. The smaller hamlets stand. The dieback is mapped, named, and slowly begins to heal in the places where the source has been removed.

end_exposure_coda.text:
Marek loses his post. He loses his pension. He loses, for a time, the use of his hands to the cold he caught the third night out, when he gave you his blanket because he thought you needed it more.

You lose the work you would have had. You lose, in the small bureaucratic ways the empire reserves for those it cannot prosecute, the easy version of your life.

A winter passes. Then another. In the spring of the third year you walk a path along the upper river with him, and the willow has come back where the willow had gone, and he stops at a bend you both remember and says, very quietly, that this was worth it.

You agree. You do not say so. He knows.

end_exposure.text: The Hollow Forest holds. So does what you carried out of it.

end_extracted_approach.text:
The two of you go south, then east, then south again. You stay off the imperial post roads. You sleep in barns and the back rooms of inns and once, for a week, in the cellar of a sympathetic miller who asks no questions and accepts no payment.

The documents stay against your ribs. They cross three jurisdictional lines without being seen. They reach, eventually, a desk where they will be safe — though safe is not the same word as effective, and the difference will become important later.

end_extracted_coda.text:
Vethara is not warned. Vethara is, by the time anyone listens to what you brought out, no longer Vethara — its people are dispersed, its bell-house silent, its name absent from the next set of imperial maps. You learn this in a tavern in the lowlands from a man who does not know he is telling you. Marek puts down his cup and walks outside and you find him an hour later sitting on a wall and you both know, without speaking, that this is the price of the pace at which you moved.

The documents will matter, in the years to come. They have not yet. What you saved is the man beside you. What you did not save is everything you did not have time to reach.

end_extracted.text: He is alive. The papers are real. The work, now, begins.

end_truth_only_approach.text:
You walk out alone. The case is against your ribs and the road is empty in front of you and behind you the forest closes the way forests close — slowly, without fanfare, as though you had never been in it at all.

You do not know what happened to him. You will not know for some time, and when you do know, it will be third-hand and partial. Whether Serrin's patience ran out. Whether Marek found, in the last hour, some way to step sideways out of the camp on his own terms. Whether he is in a cell, in a grave, or sitting in some lowland cottage under a different name reading a different newspaper.

end_truth_only_coda.text:
You do what he would have asked you to do, because you are now the only one carrying it. You take the documents to people who can use them. You answer the questions you can answer. You go on record under your own name when going on record is what is needed.

It is not enough. It was never going to be enough. The forest goes on dying for another season before the slowest parts of the imperial machine begin to turn. Some of the smaller hamlets are absorbed before the documents are entered into evidence.

But some of them are not. And in a hearing room two years on, a man you do not know reads the third page aloud, and the room goes very quiet, and you sit at the back and think of a man wrapped in a grey blanket holding a tin cup he did not drink from.

end_truth_only.text: The truth survives. It is not what he would have called victory. You think, sometimes, that he would have called it enough.

end_survivor_approach.text:
You come out of the forest. You sit, eventually, in a roadhouse forty miles south, and you eat hot food, and you drink a cup of something that is not tea, and you tell the man behind the bar that the upper forest is dying.

He nods. He has heard. People come through. Hunters, surveyors. The forest has been bad two seasons running.

You tell him it is not natural. He nods again, in the way men nod at things they cannot do anything about.

end_survivor_coda.text:
Three months later you are still saying it. To a clerk at a ministry desk who takes notes and files them somewhere. To an old colleague of Marek's who listens, kindly, and asks if you have proof, and accepts your honest answer that you do not.

The forest gets quieter. The dieback widens. Marek is, as far as anyone can tell you, still missing. The empire's records describe him as a presumed-deceased survey officer last seen in the Northern Marches. The file does not close. The file does not need to close. The file simply ceases to be a file anyone consults.

You are alive. That is what you have. Some seasons it will feel like a great deal. Other seasons it will feel like very little.

end_survivor.text: You came out of the forest. The forest is still there.

end_tool_approach.text:
Serrin walks the last mile with you personally. You and Marek both. He is, as ever, easy company. He talks about a vineyard his cousin runs in the southern provinces. He laughs at his own anecdote about a bear that once walked through a survey camp in his second year. At the crossroads he stops, shakes Marek's hand, then yours, and says it has been a difficult few days but you both did very well.

He means it. That is the part that will, in the years to come, be hardest to carry.

end_tool_coda.text:
The report you did not write was written by someone else. The route you took is on a map now, in an office in the capital, with a small annotation in a margin: passable on foot, late autumn, with light supplies. The cache you ate from is restocked. The markers you followed have been extended a further fifteen miles north.

Marek goes home. He does not speak much, in the months that follow. He does not, ever, ask you what you saw in the forester's hut, because you did not go to the forester's hut, because the route they walked you out on did not pass it.

A year later you read in a regional paper that the Vethara settlement has been formally dissolved due to ongoing population decline. You sit with the paper in your lap for a long time. Then you fold it and put it in a drawer with the other papers you have folded and put away. There are a number of them now.

end_tool.text: You walked out of the forest. The forest, in walking you out, made use of you.

death_agents.text:
Serrin kneels beside you in the wet bracken and opens your jacket and removes the case with the unhurried care of a man who has, in a long career, done this a small number of times.

He is not pleased. He is not displeased. He is, as he has been throughout, professional.

You should have walked out with us, he says again, very quietly. He closes your jacket. He stands. He gestures to the rifleman, who begins to do the work of making this look like something it was not.

The forest does not record what is done in it. That is the operation's first principle. It is also, you understand at the very end, the worst thing about him.

h_2a.text:
The packframe man stands as you reach the level ground. He is younger than the others — late twenties, perhaps, with the spare build of a man who walks for a living. He nods at you civilly and steps aside to let you pass. His hand never leaves the strap of the framepack, and the framepack is closer to the second tent than it needs to be for the work he was doing.

You note all of this without changing your face. Marek does not stand. The blanket is grey wool, regulation issue, the kind of blanket the Northern Survey provides to its officers and to no one else.

h_3a.text:
The man at the packframe is not, you decide, sorting straps. He is performing the act of sorting straps. The same buckle has gone through his hands four times. His attention is on the second tent, the one you cannot see into.

The second tent has a fly rigged out from its eastern face — the kind of awning a man rigs when he intends to sit under it and write, and does not want the wind on his pages. There is a folding camp table beneath the fly. There is a leather despatch case on the table, beside an oil lamp not yet lit.

You file all of this away.

h_3b.text:
The light shifts. The wind, which had been carrying away from you, drops for a long moment and then returns. The rifleman's head turns half a degree and you flatten yourself further into the bracken and the moment passes.

It is time to stop watching. Either you go down or you go around.

h_4a.text: Halfway down the slope you stop fitting the bracken to your boots and start walking openly, because openly is now the only thing left to be. The rifleman tracks you all the way in. Serrin stands and waits, hands at his sides, the smile arriving slightly before you do.

h_5a.text:
The ground north of the camp is harder than the slope you came in on. Old windfall, cross-cut by a deer track that no longer sees deer. You move slowly. You stop twice when the wind shifts and once when something moves in the underbrush that turns out to be a vole.

It takes you longer than you would like.

h_5b.text:
From the lip of the draw you have a clean line down to the back of the second tent. The fly is rigged. The despatch case is on the table. Serrin's voice carries faintly from the fire — something he is reading aloud to Marek, in the conversational tone of a man who knows he will not be interrupted.

The stream is below you. The path to the stream comes up between the tents and goes down past your position.

choices.text: Wait. Marek will come for water.

h_6a.text:
He is back at the fire before you have moved. Serrin says something. Marek says something brief in return. The kettle is set on the stones. The conversation resumes.

Whatever happens next, the window has closed. He has used the moment he had.

choices.text: Walk into the camp anyway. Whatever this is, do it together.

h_10a.text:
Serrin is, in his small movements, a man entirely at ease. He is not performing ease. He has the bearing of someone whose superiority of position is so complete he has never had cause to think about it. He pours tea with one hand, gestures with the other, and when he laughs it is a real laugh — short, open, immediately gone.

The rifleman does not turn. The packframe man has resumed his straps. Marek's eyes, you notice, do not leave the fire.

h_11a.text:
You hold the cup. The bergamot is real bergamot. The other thing — the herbal note you cannot name — is faint enough that you would not be sure of it if you were not looking for it. It might be nothing. It might be valerian, or hop, or one of half a dozen things a careful man might add to a tea offered to a tired guest.

You do not drink. You hold the cup as though you were about to drink, and you wait.

h_12a.text:
From your standing position you can see Marek's hands around his cup. They are steady, but they are steady in the particular way of a man who has decided to make them so. The knuckles of his right hand are raw. He has been gripping things — pencils, blanket-edges, his own forearm — harder than he needs to.

He has been here longer than three days.

h_20a.text:
The packframe man has gone into the second tent to fetch something. The rifleman is rolling his shoulders against the cold. Serrin is bent over the map, finger tracing a tributary, brow furrowed in genuine concentration over what is, you suspect, an entirely unimportant line.

The window is open.

h_31.text:
Night settles. The stars come out cold above the camp. Serrin tells a story about a survey he ran in the southern hills, twenty years ago, and laughs at his own punchline, and the rifleman laughs too — the first sound you have heard the rifleman make. The fire is good. The stew, when it comes, is good.

Marek eats. He eats slowly, and he chews thoroughly, and when Serrin offers him a second helping he accepts. He is, in his way, performing the wellness Serrin requires of him.

You take a bowl and you eat what you can. You sleep, eventually, in the spare tent, with your knife under the rolled jacket that serves as your pillow. You do not dream.

h_35a.text:
Serrin lets you have the silence. He is good at silence — better than most men are at conversation. He waits beside you until the wind shifts and then, very softly, as though the matter were already settled, says: think it over. There is no hurry. We will speak again before dawn.

He walks back toward the fire. He does not look back.

choices.text: You know exactly what he is asking. (You understand the contamination.)

h_38a.text:
The bracken closes behind you. Forty paces in, you crouch and wait and listen. No one follows. Not yet. They are confident enough in the offer Serrin made you to give you the hour.

You will not be back inside the hour.

h_38b.text: The choice is the same as it was at the fire, in different clothes. Northeast, where Marek pointed. North, where your own legs would carry you. The light is going. Whatever you choose, you will be choosing in the half-hour before dark.

choices.text: Northeast — follow the dieback to its source.

h_45a.text:
You do not stay long at the foot of the beech. The sky is closing in, and there is more light in the west than you would have thought, and the more you sit the harder it will be to start again.

You stand. You take a bearing off the lowering sun. Northeast.

h_48a.text:
You sit a moment with the case across your knees. The hut is silent. A jay calls somewhere outside. The light through the hole in the roof is the colour of tea.

You fold the seal back over its broken cord — for what it is worth — and slide the cord into your pocket. You do not know why. Perhaps because it is still, even broken, a thing that means something.

h_50a.text: You go fast at first, then slower as your legs remember what they have been doing for a week. The track points west toward the river and you do not take it. The track points east toward the camp and you do not take it. There is a third way, north through the standing birches, where no track exists because no track is needed.

h_55a.text:
The reading does not, as you might have thought, make the world clearer. It makes the world heavier.

You understand, now, the size of the thing in your hands. You understand what was taken from Marek by the men who built that camp. You understand what is owed to the small communities downstream of what is in the soil.

You close the case. You stand.

choices.text: Find Serrin. Make him say it.
Out. Get this to the world that can use it.

h_60a.text:
The street is short. Twenty houses, maybe. A communal byre at the lower end. The bell-house at the upper. Children somewhere out of sight, calling to one another in the upper pasture. Smoke from three chimneys, no more than that, on a winter afternoon when there should have been smoke from twenty.

The village has been thinning. They have not yet given a name to what is thinning them. You have come with a name.

choices.text: Tell her what you know. Ask for the headman.
Ask only for water. You are too tired to do this properly.

h_65a.text:
He does not raise his voice once. He explains the funding cycle. He explains the demographic projections. He explains, with a kind of dry honest weariness, that he himself did not design the operation but that he agrees, on balance, that it is preferable to the alternatives the planning committee was considering in its earlier sessions.

This is the thing you have to hold onto: he believes what he is saying. The evil of him is not that he is lying. The evil of him is that the lie has been told upstream, by men he respects, and he is the place where the lie meets the forest.

h_65b.text:
When he is done, he sits back on his stool and looks at you across the fire and waits. He has given you the long answer. He believes you owe him the courtesy of a considered response.

Marek is still wrapped in the grey blanket. He is no longer pretending to look at the embers. He is looking at you.

h_68a.text:
The rendezvous is a stand of three old larches on a knuckle of rock half a mile north of the camp. You picked it on the way out — Marek would know to come here, if he were coming. You wait.

The wind is up. You can hear, very faintly, the camp behind you — the murmur of men who are not yet aware that anything has gone wrong.

h_69a.text:
He hands you the compass. You take it. He hands you a strip of dried meat from his satchel and says, eat it now, you will not want to stop later, and you eat it now.

We go, he says. We do not stop until the upper ford. If I fall behind, you do not wait. You take the case and you go.

You tell him you will not leave him behind. He looks at you a moment and nods, and you both understand that the agreement was a courtesy on both sides.

h_70a.text:
You wait one more quarter hour, because you have to, because not waiting would be a thing you would carry the rest of your life. He does not come.

What you carry, you carry alone.

h_72a.text:
The first hour out is the worst. The dieback on the western shoulder is heavier than on any ground you walked in Acts before this — the moss is brown to its roots, the birches stand like bones, and where you put your boot the ground gives a half-inch more than it should.

This is the centre of what they did. You are walking through the success of the operation.

h_73a.text: On the far bank you sit a long minute and pour the water out of your boots. Your hands are shaking with cold but they are shaking, which is better than not shaking. You stand. The track on this side leads up through living birches. You take it.

h_75a.text:
Twice, in the long dark, Marek stops and lifts a hand and you both go still. Boots on the ridge, perhaps, or perhaps only the wind in dead branches. Each time, after a long minute, he lowers the hand and you go on.

They are not on this track. They never were.

h_76a.text:
Twice your boot goes through the moss into a hollow no surface deer would have sprung. Twice you catch yourself against a dead trunk. The trunks are not always sound. Once, a section of bark comes away in your hand and you nearly go down.

You keep walking because stopping is a worse choice than walking.

h_80a.text:
You walk a final hour with the road firm under you. The sound of your boots on the gravel is the first ordinary sound you have heard in a week. You do not, at first, recognise it as ordinary.

A crow goes overhead, calling, and the call is clean — no answer comes back, because there are no other crows on this stretch of road, but the absence is the absence of an ordinary forest, not the absence of life itself. You have crossed back into the world that is still working.

h_55b.text:
You find a folded sheet at the bottom of the case that is not on Survey letterhead. It is a personal letter from one of the senior names on the operation, written to another of the senior names, in a hand that has the looseness of long acquaintance.

The letter is, by the standards of what surrounds it, not damning. It is worse than damning. It is casual. The two men whose names you are reading have been corresponding about this for years. There are jokes. There are references to a shared boat. There is a postscript about a wife's recovery from an illness.

You put the letter back in the case last, on top of everything else.

h_61a.text: She walks you to the bell-house herself. The headman is mending a harness in the lee of the wall. He stands when he sees her face. He does not yet know what he is about to be told, but he knows, by the way she walks, that it will require him to stop mending the harness.

h_67a.text: You lie awake the whole night. Twice you hear soft footsteps near your tent and once the flap is briefly lifted — by the wind, you tell yourself, by the wind. Before first light you are gathering your few things in the dark, slowly, slowly. The camp is silent. The sentries have shifted.

h_77a.text:
Serrin walks at your shoulder down off the spur. He talks, quietly, almost kindly. He tells you that you came very close. He tells you that he respects what you did. He tells you that he wishes, for what it is worth, that things had gone differently, and you understand that the wishing is not a thing he is performing for your benefit.

It is the worst part of him.

h_42.text:
Halfway to the hut you stop and look back. The camp is below the rise, invisible from where you stand, but you can see in your mind exactly what is happening there — Serrin's small recalibration, the rifleman shifting his weight, Marek by the fire registering the texture of the silence.

You turn and walk on.

h_43.text:
The track to the hut crosses a small creek with a rotten plank for a bridge. You test the plank. It holds. You cross.

On the far side a length of wire is staked into the ground at ankle height. Old wire — corroded, half-grown over. Not a tripwire, you decide after a moment's careful inspection. The remains of a fence, from the years when the forester had a garden here. You step over it.

h_44.text:
The first thing you see when the hut comes into view is that someone has been here recently. Not the agents — the disturbance is too slight, the leaves only just beginning to settle back into the path. Marek, before the camp. He has been here, opened the cavity, replaced the stone, and gone on.

He knew, then. He came back here on his own. The case has been waiting for whoever could reach it next.

h_76b.text:
You crest the ridge in the grey hour, legs gone soft with the night's walking, and stop.

Below you, on the only track that leads off the dieback and onto living ground, a man is standing.

The rifleman. He has had time to choose his ground and he has chosen it well. The track narrows to a gap between two outcroppings and he is in the gap, facing up the slope. He is watching you come.

He does not call out.

h_76c.text:
You set your feet on the slope. He watches you do it with the patient attention of someone who has made this calculation before and knows how it comes out.

He fights without theatre — no warning, no pause for negotiation. He comes up the slope to meet you, which is the correct tactical decision, and you understand in the first exchange that he is better trained than the desperate man in the forest, better trained perhaps than you. He does not hate you. He is simply working.

h_76d.text:
You break back up the slope into the dieback. He follows twenty paces, maybe thirty, and stops — not because he cannot go further, but because the sky is beginning to grey and the ground is bad and someone will want an accounting.

You go north, then west in a long circuit. The hard way around the ridge. The footing is worse than the direct route. You do not fall. It costs you the better part of another hour and what is left of your reserves.

h_74_caught.text: The boots that come up the ridge are Serrin's man at the head of them. He does not say anything. He does not need to.

h_77b.text:
Serrin's man is professional. He goes through your pack with systematic patience. He checks the lining of your jacket. He straightens up and says nothing to you — because there is nothing to say. You are not carrying what he was sent to find.

He walks you back down to the camp. Serrin is waiting at the fire and reads the situation in his man's face before either of you speaks.

Nothing, Serrin says, after a moment. It was not a question. He looks at you with the mild, slightly regretful expression of a man revising a calculation.

Come and sit down, he says. There is still tea.

h_76_collapse.text:
By the grey hour you are still in the dieback, and your ankle has gone, and the stars are wrong above you because cloud has come in and the cold is a weight now rather than a temperature.

You sit down to rest. You tell yourself it is for a moment.

You are found on the second morning by a forester working the edge of the dieback — a young man with a mule and the good sense to recognise the difference between a dead person and a person who is about to be. He does not ask questions. He gets you onto the mule and gets you out.

You are not carrying much. You are not sure, in the grey hours of the first day, how much of what you remember is real.
